- 1 John 3:11-24 — Love in action: sacrificial love and confidence before God.  
- 1 John 4:1-6 — Test the spirits: discern true vs false teaching.  
- 1 John 4:7-21 — God is love: love’s origin, Christ’s love, and the call to love one another (love, hypostatic union implicit in incarnation).  
- 1 John 5:1-12 — Faith in the Son of God: testimony concerning Jesus and life through him.  
- 1 John 5:13-21 — Assurance and closing exhortations: know you have eternal life; guard against idols and sin (assurance, perseverance).

Major themes and theological significance (concise)
- Incarnation and Christology: John insists on the real, historical, incarnate Jesus as decisive for fellowship and truth (1 John 1:1-2; 1 John 4:2). This safeguards the hypostatic union and the reality of Christ’s saving work.  
- Atonement and advocacy: Jesus is presented both as the atoning sacrifice and as our Advocate before the Father, grounding forgiveness and assurance (1 John 2:1-2; 1 John 4:10).  
- Assurance of salvation: Believers can know they have eternal life by faith in the Son and by the marks of new birth—love, obedience, and righteous living (1 John 5:13; 1 John 3:9-10).  
- Love as the ethic of truth: Love for God and neighbor proves true belief; doctrine and devotion are inseparable (1 John 4:7-12; 1 John 3:16-18).  
- Testing doctrine and life: John gives practical tests—belief about Jesus, moral fruit, love for brothers, and obedience to God—to discern genuine faith from false profession (1 John 4:1-3; 1 John 3:14).

Key practical applications (one actionable next step)
- Read 1 John through slowly over 7–10 days, journaling two questions each day: "How does this passage show who Jesus is?" and "What one concrete act of love or repentance should I do today?" Memorize and pray over 1 John 1:9; 1 John 4:19; 1 John 5:13 as anchors. Then share what God is teaching you with a trusted believer or your local elder for accountability.

Suggested key verses to learn and use in ministry or pastoral care
- 1 John 1:9; 1 John 4:7-12; 1 John 5:13.

Would you like a printable chapter-by-chapter reading plan with short reflection questions, or a short sermon-style outline for each chapter? I can prepare either.
What happens at the final judgment?

At the final judgment Jesus Christ will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead. Every person will be raised and will stand before Christ to receive the righteous verdict of God, which results in either eternal life in the renewed creation or eternal condemnation. This is not a private, symbolic event but the decisive public vindication of God’s justice and the completion of salvation for his people.

Key elements, with Scripture:
- Christ’s return and role as judge, appointed by the Father, is central, Matthew 25:31-46; Acts 17:31.  
- The dead will be raised, the righteous to life and the wicked to condemnation, John 5:28-29; 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.  
- All must give an account before Christ, and each will be judged according to what they have done, Romans 14:10-12; 2 Corinthians 5:10.  
- The books are opened and the Book of Life is searched; those not found written there face final condemnation and the lake of fire, Revelation 20:11-15.  
- For God’s people the end is glorification and entrance into the new heavens and new earth, where God dwells with his people and there is no more death, Revelation 21:1-4; 1 Corinthians 15:51-54.  
- The certainty that people die once and then face judgment underlines the urgency, Hebrews 9:27.

Theologically: this final event completes justification and results in glorification for those who are in Christ, while revealing the righteous wrath of God against sin for those who remain unbelieving. Our standing before the Judge is based on Christ’s atoning work applied to us by faith, not by our own merit, because all have sinned, Romans 3:23; the wages of sin is death, but God’s gift is eternal life in Christ Jesus, Romans 6:23; and Christ died and was raised for our justification, 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 5:8-10.

Pastoral, practical next step: if you are not trusting Jesus Christ for forgiveness and acceptance before God, turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Read the Gospel of John and Romans, speak with a Bible-teaching pastor, and join a local church where you can be instructed, baptized (if you are a believer), and fed.

If you want, tell me where you are on this—do you already trust Christ, or are you wondering whether you should?
In what ways is becoming a Christian becoming an entirely new man / woman?

Yes — becoming a Christian really does make a person a new man or woman. At conversion God begins several real, interconnected changes: legal standing before God, inner life, identity, desires, community, and mission.

Short summary (one sentence): By faith you are declared righteous (justification), born again (regeneration), adopted as God’s child, united to Christ, and set on a progressive path of sanctification so that your whole life is renewed (2 Corinthians 5:17; Romans 3:24; Titus 3:5; Romans 8:1; Galatians 2:20).

What that renewal looks like (brief points with Scripture):
- New legal status — you are forgiven and counted righteous through Christ’s work (justification, imputed righteousness). Romans 3:24; Romans 5:1.  
- New birth and new nature — God gives a new heart and Spirit who makes you alive to God (regeneration). John 3:3-8; Titus 3:5; Ezekiel 36:26.  
- Union with Christ — you are baptized into his death and resurrection; your identity is now “in Christ.” Romans 6:3-5; 2 Corinthians 5:17.  
- Indwelling Spirit and inner change — the Holy Spirit renews your desires, convicts of sin, empowers obedience, and produces Christlike fruit. Romans 8:9; 1 Corinthians 6:19; Galatians 5:22-23.  
- New affections and freedom from condemnation — sin’s power is broken and your desires begin to change, though struggle remains (the already/not-yet of sanctification). Romans 8:1; Philippians 2:13.  
- New family and fellowship — you enter a church family where you are loved, taught, corrected, and sent. Ephesians 2:19; Acts 2:42-47.  
- New purpose and hope — you have a mission (make disciples) and living hope in Christ’s resurrection and the coming renewal of all things. Matthew 28:19-20; 1 Peter 1:3-4; Romans 8:11.

A clear gospel bridge: Christians believe every person sins and falls short of God’s glory, Christ died and rose to pay for sin and to give new life, and forgiveness and new birth are received by repentance and faith alone in Christ (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

One practical next step: if you’re new or wanting renewal, begin reading the Gospel of John (one chapter a day) asking the Holy Spirit to show you Jesus, tell a mature Christian or your pastor you want discipleship, and ask them to pray with you and help you toward baptism and a local church home.

Would you like a short reading plan (first 30 days) or a few verses to memorize that help shape a new identity in Christ?
What are the Pauline epistles?

The Pauline epistles are the thirteen New Testament letters traditionally attributed to the Apostle Paul: Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon. Paul identifies himself as an apostle in letters such as Romans 1:1 and 1 Corinthians 1:1.

These epistles teach central Christian doctrines—justification by faith, sanctification, the atoning work and resurrection of Christ, and the life of the church—which you can see especially in Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8-9; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. They are part of the canon Christians receive as Scripture and are read and applied as such (see 2 Timothy 3:16).

Practical step: if you want to begin reading, start with Romans and read one chapter a day, praying for the Holy Spirit to illuminate the text and discuss what you learn with a local church or Bible study. 

Note: Hebrews was historically sometimes attributed to Paul but its authorship is disputed, so it is often treated separately from the Pauline corpus.
How can I be sure I am praying according to the will of God?

Short answer: you can be confident you are praying according to God’s will when your prayer is grounded in Scripture, submitted to God’s sovereignty, shaped by the Spirit, and motivated by obedience and love rather than selfish desires. 1 John 5:14–15 BSB gives the basic promise: "This is the confidence we have before him: if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us. And if we know that he hears us—whatever we ask—we know that we have what we asked of him."

What that looks like practically
- Pray Scripture back to God, because God’s revealed will is found in his Word (for example, pray the Lord’s Prayer and petitions like "Thy kingdom come; thy will be done," Matthew 6:10 BSB).  
- Depend on the Holy Spirit to shape your praying and intercede for you, especially when you don’t know how to pray, Romans 8:26–27 BSB.  
- Submit your requests to God’s sovereign wisdom as Jesus modeled: "Not my will, but yours," Luke 22:42 BSB.  
- Examine your heart for unconfessed sin or wrong motives, since God sometimes withholds answers when our motives are selfish, James 4:3 BSB, and obeying Christ’s commands helps our requests align with God’s delight, 1 John 3:22 BSB.  
- Pray in Jesus’ name—asking in his name ties our requests to the work and authority of Christ, John 14:13–14 BSB.

How to tell if a specific prayer is answered according to God’s will
- The answer honors God’s character and Scripture, brings spiritual fruit (growth in holiness, love, gospel advance), and often comes with inner peace or wise counsel; ultimately God’s timing and purpose—not immediate feeling—are decisive, 1 John 5:14–15 BSB; Romans 8:26–27 BSB.

One simple next step to practice this
- For seven days, before you petition, spend one minute confessing known sin (Psalm 51 style), one minute reading a short promise or petition from Scripture (for example, Ephesians 1:15–23 or Psalm 23), then pray that passage back to God and end with, "Father, do what is best—your will, not mine." Ask the Spirit to guide you each time, Romans 8:26–27 BSB. If you’re aware of persistent sin or confusion, ask a trusted elder or mature believer to pray with you.

Encouragement
God delights to hear his children and to conform our desires to his will as we grow in sanctification; therefore keep coming to the throne of grace with confidence and humility, Hebrews 4:16 BSB. If you want, tell me a specific prayer you’re wrestling with and I’ll help you pray it Scripture-shaped and Spirit-led.
What does the Bible say about busyness / being too busy?

Brief answer: The Bible warns that sinful, anxious, or excessive busyness can crowd out our devotion to Christ and lead to weary, fruitless labor; it calls us to order our work and time under God’s priorities, practice Sabbath rest, and trust Jesus for rest and wisdom. Luke 10:41-42; Matthew 6:25-34; Matthew 11:28-30.

Key biblical teachings, with short notes
- Jesus rebukes distracted busyness, calling us to sit at his feet first, not merely to be “distracted and worried about many things.” This is the heart of the Mary and Martha incident. Luke 10:41-42.
- God warns against anxious worry about daily needs and calls us to trust his Fatherly provision rather than frantic activity. Matthew 6:25-34; 1 Peter 5:7.
- Jesus invites the weary to come and find rest in him, showing that true rest is gospel-centered, not merely a slower pace. Matthew 11:28-30.
- God intends rest and rhythm for people, signaled in the Sabbath command and reinforced by Jesus’ teaching that the Sabbath was made for man. Exodus 20:8-11; Mark 2:27.
- Work apart from God’s blessing can be empty and wearisome; Solomon laments toil that leaves the heart unsatisfied. Ecclesiastes 2:22-23; Psalm 127:1-2.
- Believers are called to set their minds on heavenly things and to remove distractions that hinder sanctification and focused discipleship. Colossians 3:2; Hebrews 12:1-2.

A brief historical/word note
- The Greek word used for the worry that plagued Martha is the same root often translated “anxious” or “to be overly concerned,” showing the Bible links busyness with anxious care that steals spiritual attention. Luke 10:41.

Concrete, pastoral applications (one actionable next step)
- Practical step to try this week: pick one 30-minute quiet time each morning (or an evening if mornings are impossible) where you stop task lists, read Luke 10 and Matthew 6, pray briefly, and simply sit with Jesus. Treat this appointment as nonnegotiable, tell one trusted friend, and protect that margin for seven days. Luke 10:41-42; Matthew 6:25-34.
- Additional helps: audit commitments (say “no” to one recurring obligation this month), build a weekly Sabbath or partial-day rest, and practice handing anxious thoughts to God when they arise by naming them and praying 1 Peter 5:7.

Pastoral encouragement
- If you feel overwhelmed, you are not failing beyond God’s help; he calls you to trust and to re-order life toward him, and Jesus himself promises rest for the weary. Matthew 11:28-30.
- If you’d like, tell me what a typical day looks like and I can help you design a simple weekly rhythm that protects margin and spiritual growth.
What are the seven baptisms mentioned in the Bible, and what do they mean?

Short answer: Scripture uses the word "baptism" for several related but distinct realities. Below are seven baptisms mentioned in the Bible, with what each means and the key verses (Berean Standard Bible).

1) John’s baptism (baptism of repentance)
- What it is: A call to repentance and preparatory water baptism, pointing people to the coming Messiah. It was not the saving baptism of the Spirit but a public confession and cleansing that prepared hearts for Christ.
- Key texts: Matthew 3:1–11; Mark 1:4.

2) Baptism of fire
- What it is: The imagery of purifying or judicial fire linked with the coming Messiah’s work—variously understood as purifying judgment or the purifying work of the Spirit in sanctification.
- Key text: Matthew 3:11.

3) Baptism with/by the Holy Spirit (Spirit baptism)
- What it is: The Spirit’s work that unites believers to Christ, regenerates, and empowers them for ministry (the inward, saving work of the Spirit). This is often tied to Pentecost and to the believer’s incorporation into the body of Christ.
- Key texts: Acts 1:5; Acts 2:4; 1 Corinthians 12:13.

4) Baptism into Christ (union with Christ)
- What it is: The doctrinal description of how believers are mystically and legally united to Christ by faith—so that we are "in Christ." Water baptism is the visible sign of this union for believers.
- Key texts: Romans 6:3–4; Galatians 3:27.

5) Baptism into death (identification with Christ’s death and resurrection)
- What it is: The theological emphasis that through baptism we are identified with Christ’s death, burial, and resurrection—signifying dying to sin and rising to new life (central to soteriology and sanctification).
- Key text: Romans 6:3–4.

6) Baptism into Moses (typological/ceremonial baptism)
- What it is: Paul’s use of the term to describe Israel’s passage through the Red Sea as a kind of corporate "baptism" into their covenant representative, Moses—an Old Testament type that foreshadows Christian union with Christ.
- Key text: 1 Corinthians 10:2.

7) Baptism for the dead (an obscure, debated reference)
- What it is: A hard and debated text where Paul mentions people being "baptized for the dead." Scholars and churches disagree about its exact meaning (a literal vicarious rite, a figurative expression, or something else), and it is not presented elsewhere as a normative Christian practice.
- Key text: 1 Corinthians 15:29.

A few clarifying notes
- Overlap: Several of these refer to the same reality seen from different angles. For example, "baptism with the Holy Spirit," "baptism into Christ," and "baptism into death" describe the same spiritual union and work of salvation from different emphases (regeneration, union with Christ, and identification with his death and resurrection). See Romans 6:3–4 and 1 Corinthians 12:13 together.
- Debated items: "Baptism of fire" and "baptism for the dead" are the most disputed in interpretation; Christians have offered different, charitable readings of those phrases while holding to essential gospel truths.

Theological terms to keep in mind: repentance, Spirit baptism (regeneration/indwelling), union with Christ, and believer’s baptism as the public ordinance signaling that union.

Practical step: If you are a believer and unsure which baptism applies to you, read Romans 6 and Acts 2, then talk with your pastor about receiving believer’s baptism by immersion as a public testimony to your union with Christ.

If you have not yet trusted Christ, these baptisms point to the same gospel truth: we are sinners in need of a Savior, and Jesus died and rose to bring forgiveness and new life—turn (repent) and trust in Jesus Christ alone for salvation. Would you like help understanding how these baptisms relate to your own faith and practice?
What do you think about the Bible codes? Is there any validity to them?

Short answer: No — Bible codes are not a reliable or valid way to read Scripture or to prove prophecy. They rest on selective pattern‑finding and statistical tricks, not on sound hermeneutics or the Bible’s own teaching about how God’s Word is given and used.

Why I say that, briefly:
- Methodological problems. Bible‑code methods usually search for equidistant letter sequences or other patterns after the fact, and then highlight hits while ignoring failures. When the same techniques are applied to other long texts (for example, Moby Dick), similar “codes” appear. That shows the effect is a statistical artifact, not a supernatural message.
- Hermeneutical problems. Biblical interpretation (hermeneutics) depends on grammar, context, authorial intent, and the Bible interpreting the Bible. Secret letter patterns bypass the plain, historical reading the apostles and prophets expected their readers to use. The Bible presents itself as Scripture for teaching, correction, and training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:15-17 BSB), and as a living Word that judges the heart (Hebrews 4:12 BSB), not as a puzzle requiring hidden codes.
- Spiritual concerns. Pursuing hidden messages can drift toward numerology or secret lore, which Scripture warns against (Deuteronomy 18:10-12 BSB). The point of God’s revelation is to lead sinners to Christ and holy living, not to reward cryptic games.

What proponents sometimes say and a brief reply:
- Claim: Codes prove divine authorship or hidden prophecy. Reply: That claim overlooks the methodological weaknesses: post‑hoc searching, selection bias, and lack of reproducible, predictive power. Genuine prophecy in the Bible is attested by fulfillment in clear historical ways and by the testimony of Scripture itself, not by letter puzzles.
- Claim: Even if controversial, maybe God could encode messages this way. Reply: God certainly could do many things, but Scripture gives us the means He intended for knowing Him — the clear proclamation of the Word and the gospel (2 Timothy 3:15-17 BSB; Psalm 119:105 BSB). We should not prefer private speculations to the means God appointed.

Practical application:
- If you’re curious, read the Bible the way the apostles intended: with attention to context, grammar, and Christ as the center (hermeneutics, sola Scriptura). A concrete next step is a short course or book on biblical interpretation (for example, an introductory guide to hermeneutics) and to practice reading a New Testament book, asking who wrote it, to whom, and why.
- If you’ve seen a specific “code” claim, I can look at it with you and point out where the patterning or the reasoning breaks down and what the plain text actually says.

Pastoral note: I understand the appeal — people long for signs that God is directly speaking now in dramatic ways. That longing is understandable, but Scripture brings the sure and saving message we need: Christ’s death and resurrection for sinners (2 Timothy 3:15-17 BSB). If you’d like, tell me a particular code claim and I’ll examine it with you, or I can recommend accessible resources on trustworthy Bible study.
How can I see the hand of God moving in my life?

Short answer: you see the hand of God when you learn to recognize his providence through Scripture, prayer, changed heart (sanctification), providential circumstances, the witness of the Spirit, and the counsel and confirmation of the church.

How to discern his work (brief, biblical signs)
- Scripture guiding and shaping your way: when your experience lines up with God's Word and brings you to obedience, that is a key sign of his hand (Proverbs 3:5-6 BSB; Hebrews 4:12 BSB).  
- Providence: God often moves by opening and closing doors, arranging circumstances, or sending people for your good—remembering that God works all things for those who love him (Romans 8:28 BSB).  
- The Holy Spirit’s inward witness and conviction: the Spirit guides, comforts, and clarifies God’s will (John 16:13 BSB; 1 John 4:1 BSB — test what you sense by Scripture).  
- Growth in holiness and the fruit of the Spirit: visible change in your character (love, joy, peace, patience, etc.) is evidence God is at work (Galatians 5:22-23 BSB; Philippians 1:6 BSB).  
- Answered prayer according to God’s will: when your requests are granted in ways that match God’s revealed will, this confirms his activity (1 John 5:14 BSB).  
- Wise counsel and church confirmation: God often confirms his direction through mature believers and gospel-centered elders (Proverbs 15:22 BSB).

Practical next step (one concrete exercise)
- For the next two weeks keep a short “Providence Journal.” Each night write 1–3 specific things from the day that looked like God’s hand (Scripture that struck you, an open/closed door, an answered prayer, growth in your heart, godly counsel). After two weeks review patterns and bring them to your pastor or a trusted Christian friend to help discern what you’ve seen.

A few cautions in love
- Don’t assume every coincidence is a direct word from God; test impressions by Scripture and church wisdom (1 John 4:1 BSB).  
- Remember God’s sovereignty doesn’t remove mystery; sometimes we must walk by faith without knowing every reason (Hebrews 11:1 BSB).

If you want, I can give a simple two-week journal template and sample nightly prompts, or help you think through an experience you’ve had and whether it looks like God’s hand. Would you like that?
Are we to love the sinner but hate the sin?

Short answer: Yes, in a biblical sense. The Bible commands us to love people while opposing and calling out their sin—loving the sinner means showing gospel mercy and seeking their repentance; hating the sin means refusing to affirm or ignore what Scripture calls sinful.

Why: Jesus shows both in practice—He loved the woman caught in adultery and told her to go and sin no more (John 8:11), and He taught love even for enemies (Matthew 5:44), while Scripture plainly calls sin what it is and urges holiness (1 John 3:4; Romans 6:1–2). We are to speak the truth in love (Ephesians 4:15) and restore sinners gently, aiming for their holiness (Galatians 6:1).

What this looks like practically
- Compassion, not approval: Offer mercy and presence without condoning sinful choices (Romans 5:8; Titus 3:3–7).  
- Honest correction, not shaming: Confront sin with gentleness and clarity, aiming for repentance and growth (Galatians 6:1; Matthew 18:15).  
- Boundaries when needed: Love sometimes requires firm limits to protect others and yourself while still seeking their good (Romans 13:4 as a principle for restraining evil in society).  
- Dependence on the Spirit: We entrust change to the Holy Spirit rather than trying to coerce repentance by anger (Philippians 2:13).

A concrete next step: Pray for the person now, asking God for wisdom and a loving, honest word you can speak; then, if appropriate, ask God to open a specific conversation in the next week where you gently point them to Christ and call them to repentance (Ephesians 6:19–20; Colossians 4:6).

Gospel reminder: We all fall short and need Christ—He died and rose again to forgive and transform sinners. Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. If you want, tell me about a specific situation and I’ll help you think through a gentle, biblical way to love that person while opposing their sin.
Is the Buddhist concept of Zen compatible with the Christian faith?

Short answer: No — Zen as a Buddhist religious system is not compatible with the Christian faith, because it rests on a different view of God, of sin, and of salvation (self-realization/enlightenment rather than justification by grace through faith in Christ). Jesus said, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6 BSB); Acts 4:12 BSB likewise affirms there is salvation in no one else.

Why, briefly:
- Zen’s ultimate goal is awakening/enlightenment by disciplined practice and a form of inner self-realization, which functions like salvation by works, not salvation by grace through faith. The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory (Romans 3:23 BSB) and that we are saved by grace through faith, not by works (Ephesians 2:8-9 BSB).
- Zen’s metaphysics and methods presuppose different ultimate reality and authority than the triune God revealed in Scripture; the Christian confession of God as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit and of Christ’s unique atoning work are non-negotiable (Matthew 28:19 BSB; Colossians 1:15-20 BSB; Hebrews 9:12 BSB).
- Because Christian faith centers on a personal, covenant God who redeems sinners through the incarnate, crucified, and risen Christ, any spiritual practice must be tested against Scripture and the person and work of Jesus (1 John 4:1 BSB; John 14:6 BSB).

A charitable nuance: some Christians distinguish between Zen as a religious system and particular practices often called "mindfulness" or quieting techniques. Christian meditation, by contrast, means silently reflecting on God’s Word and seeking God’s presence (Psalm 1:2 BSB; Joshua 1:8 BSB; Psalm 119:15 BSB). Two historic approaches among Christians are: (1) some accept carefully adapted silence and breathing practices when they are explicitly re-centered on Christ and Scripture, and (2) others reject any practices rooted in Buddhist spirituality as syncretistic and spiritually risky. My pastoral counsel (in line with Reformed Baptist caution) is: do not adopt Zen teachings or techniques that presuppose Buddhist metaphysics or seek enlightenment apart from Christ; instead, cultivate biblical meditation and disciplines—brief daily Scripture meditation and prayer, and accountability with a mature Christian leader.

One practical next step: for the next week, set aside 5–10 minutes each morning to read one verse of Scripture slowly (for example, Psalm 23 or Psalm 1), repeat it quietly in your mind, ask the Holy Spirit to illumine it, and then speak one short prayer asking God to shape your heart by that Word. Also talk with your pastor or an elder before experimenting with practices drawn from non-Christian religions.

If this raises questions about salvation, guilt, or how to pray and meditate on Scripture, I’m glad to help—are you exploring Zen for spiritual help, or trying to evaluate a specific practice? 

If you’re wondering about your standing before God: the Bible teaches we are sinners in need of a Savior, and that Christ died and rose for our justification—turn (repent) and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life.
What is the Wailing Wall / Western Wall?

The Western Wall, often called the Wailing Wall (Hebrew: the Kotel), is the western retaining wall of the Temple Mount in Jerusalem — a surviving, visible section of the massive Herodian-era work that surrounded the Jewish Second Temple complex. It is the closest publicly accessible place to where the Temple’s sanctuary once stood, and after the Romans destroyed the Temple in 70, the wall became a focal point for Jewish prayer, mourning for the loss of the Temple, and national remembrance.

Why it matters biblically and historically:
- The Temple was central to Old Covenant worship as the place of God’s dwelling presence and the site of sacrifices (see 1 Kings 8:10-11).  
- Jesus and the New Testament treat the Temple’s destruction and its meaning as the end of that Temple age and as connected to the coming of the new covenant in Christ (see Matthew 24:1-2; John 2:19).  
- The wall’s role as a place of prayer and longing for restoration reflects themes of covenant, lament, and hope found in Scripture (see Ezra 3:10-13; Psalm 122:6).

Some brief practical notes:
- The name “Wailing Wall” arose in the 19th century from observers noting Jewish mourning at the site; many today prefer “Western Wall” or “the Kotel” as less pejorative terms.  
- Visitors often place written prayers in the joints between the stones; the site is also highly sensitive politically and religiously because it adjoins the Temple Mount, a place governed and venerated by multiple faith communities.

If you’d like, I can summarize the archaeological evidence for the wall, explain how Christians read the Temple’s significance in light of Christ, or point to reliable books and articles for further reading.
What is Taizé worship, and is it biblical?

Brief definition: Taizé worship is a simple, meditative liturgical style developed by the Taizé community, an ecumenical monastic fellowship in Taizé, France, founded by Brother Roger in the 1940s. It centers on short, repetitive chants (often Scripture-based), sung responses in many languages, periods of silence, Scripture readings, brief reflections, intercession, candlelight, and communal prayer.

What it emphasizes and why people like it
- Unity and reconciliation across denominations, contemplative silence, and Scripture-shaped song, aimed at fostering inward prayer, peace, and corporate fellowship.
- Theologically it tends toward liturgical simplicity and contemplative prayer, and it prizes embodied, congregational singing that aids meditation and devotion.

Is it biblical?
- Many elements of Taizé worship are consonant with Scripture: corporate singing and teaching (Ephesians 5:19 BSB; Colossians 3:16 BSB), prayer and intercession (Philippians 4:6 BSB), reading and meditating on Scripture (Psalm 1:2 BSB), silence and waiting on the Lord (Psalm 46:10 BSB), and meeting together for worship and mutual encouragement (Hebrews 10:24-25 BSB). Worship in spirit and truth is required (John 4:24 BSB).
- Concerns to weigh pastorally: because Taizé services are short on expository preaching and often prioritize experience and repetition, they can become vague about essential doctrine unless anchored by clear Scripture teaching; the practice of contemplative silence can also be confused with non-Christian forms of meditation unless Christ-centered and Gospel-saturated (Acts 2:42 BSB; 1 Thessalonians 5:21 BSB).
- Bottom line: Taizé-style worship is not inherently unbiblical; it contains many biblically faithful elements and can be a helpful devotional form, but its biblicalness depends on whether the content and leadership maintain clear Gospel-centered doctrine and scriptural teaching.

How historic Christians differ (tertiary/secondary clarity)
- Positive view in one sentence: Many Christians appreciate Taizé because it cultivates reverent, Scripture-based prayer and simple sacramental liturgy that fosters sanctification and unity.
- Critical view in one sentence: Other Christians worry it downplays expository preaching and doctrinal clarity, opening the door to emotionalism or ambiguous theology.
- My position in one sentence: Taizé worship can be a healthy, biblical practice when used alongside solid expository preaching and catechesis, because worship forms must always be tested and sustained by Scripture and the clear proclamation of the Gospel.

One practical next step
- Try attending a Taizé service with a Bible and a pastor or mature believer, listen for whether Scripture and the Gospel of Christ are central, and afterward discuss what affirmed or troubled you; also test elements by Scripture (for example, Ephesians 5:19 BSB; Colossians 3:16 BSB; John 4:24 BSB).

If you want, tell me whether you attend a church that uses Taizé elements and I can suggest how to evaluate specific features there or give a short Scripture-based checklist to use.
Does the Old Testament truly predict a second advent of the Messiah?

Yes. The Old Testament contains promises and patterns that point both to the Messiah’s first coming in humiliation and to a future, glorious second advent.

Some read the OT as speaking only of a single, unified arrival or of Israel’s immediate restoration, arguing that the notion of two comings is a later Christian partitioning of texts. 

My position (one faithful, historic Christian reading), briefly: the OT, especially when read with New Testament revelation, presents a twofold messianic expectation—suffering and then reigning—which the New Testament reads as fulfilled in Christ’s first coming and as pointing forward to his return. For example, the Suffering Servant language that the New Testament applies to Jesus shows the humiliation of the first coming (Isaiah 53:3-6; Psalm 22:1-18), while other passages anticipate a future, worldwide rule of the anointed one (Isaiah 9:6-7; Daniel 7:13-14; Zechariah 14:4-9). The apostles themselves interpret the OT this way (Luke 24:25-27; Luke 24:44; Acts 3:18-21), giving theological coherence to the “already/not yet” shape of messianic prophecy. Theologically this connects to the doctrines of Christ’s atoning work (penal substitution in his suffering) and his future, visible return (the second advent).

Practical step: read Isaiah 53 and Zechariah 14 this week, then discuss what you notice about suffering and coming glory with a pastor or a Bible study group as a way of grounding faith in Scripture. 

If you’re unsure what this means for you spiritually: Christians believe we stand guilty before a holy God, forgiven only through Christ’s atoning death and vindicated by his resurrection, and we are called to repent and trust Jesus now while we await his return (Acts 3:19; Romans 10:9). Would you like help reading one of those Old Testament passages with a guided set of questions?
Contemporary Christian music - is it honoring to God? Should it be used in church services?

Short answer: Yes—contemporary Christian music can honor God and be used in church services when it is theologically sound, Christ‑centered, and ordered to edify the congregation. Colossians 3:16; Ephesians 5:19; John 4:23-24.

Another historic view, stated briefly: Some Christians, following a strict regulative principle, argue worship should use only Scripture‑prescribed elements and historically traditional forms, so contemporary styles are inappropriate. 1 Corinthians 14:26-40.

My position and why (one clear sentence plus brief reason): I believe churches may use contemporary music selectively, provided songs faithfully teach biblical truth, call sinners to Christ, promote sanctification and corporate worship, and are governed by pastoral oversight, because Scripture prioritizes worship that is God‑glorifying in doctrine and heart, and church practice must be ordered for the edification of all. John 4:23-24; Colossians 3:16; Romans 12:1; 1 Corinthians 14:26.

Practical criteria to decide whether contemporary songs honor God (concrete application):
- Truth, not cliché: Does the lyric accurately teach the gospel and avoid error or vague sentiment? (Colossians 3:16)
- Christ‑centeredness: Is Jesus explicit as Savior and Lord, not merely “God” as an abstract force? (Philippians 2:9-11)
- Worshipful aim: Does it lead hearts to genuine praise and devotion, not mere entertainment? (John 4:23-24)
- Congregational edification: Can ordinary people sing it together and be built up? (1 Corinthians 14:26)
- Pastoral oversight: Are elders/worship leaders shepherding song selection responsibly? (Hebrews 13:17)

A simple next step you can use in your church: ask your worship leader and elders to adopt a short song‑evaluation checklist based on the criteria above, trial new songs in small gatherings, and review them after two or three uses. For help, I can draft a one‑page checklist or evaluate 3 songs you’re considering.

If you want, tell me whether you’re a worship leader, elder, or congregant and give one or two songs you’re wondering about, and I’ll assess them briefly against these criteria.
What does it mean to test the spirits?

Brief answer: To "test the spirits" means to exercise spiritual discernment—to evaluate prophetic words, teachers, or any purported spiritual influence—to determine whether they come from the Holy Spirit or from false spirits. 1 John calls Christians to do this because truth and error often sound alike (1 John 4:1–6 BSB).

How to test the spirits practically and biblically
- Doctrinal test: Does the message confess Jesus Christ rightly? True spirits confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh and acknowledge the Father and the Son; false spirits deny that (1 John 4:2–3 BSB; 1 John 2:22–23 BSB).
- Scripture test: Does the teaching agree with the apostolic gospel and the whole testimony of Scripture? The apostles warned that a different gospel or false prophecy must be rejected (Galatians 1:8 BSB; Acts 17:11 BSB).
- Fruit test: What kind of fruit follows—holiness, humility, love, and the fruit of the Spirit—or divisiveness, greed, and immorality? Jesus and John both point to fruit as evidence (Matthew 7:15–20 BSB; Galatians 5:22–23 BSB).
- Community test: Are mature, biblically-minded elders and the local church accountable to the person or message, or is the claim independent of Scripture and church oversight? Scripture values wise, tested leadership and accountability (see Acts 17:11 BSB for the Bereans' example).

One concrete next step
- Next time you hear a prophetic word or sermon, write down its core claims, then ask: "Does this exalt Jesus and match Scripture?" Look up a few key Bible verses and compare, and then discuss it with a mature believer or elder (Acts 17:11 BSB; James 1:5 BSB).

A pastoral note
- Don’t be naively credulous, but also avoid cynical rejection; test with humility and prayer, asking God for discernment (James 1:5 BSB). If you want, bring a specific teaching or prophecy here and I’ll help you compare it with Scripture.
Why should I get married?

You should consider marriage when God calls you to a covenantal, lifelong partnership that will bless you and others, help you grow in holiness, and display Christ’s relationship to his church—but marriage is a good gift, not an absolute requirement, and singleness is also a faithful calling for some. Genesis shows God’s design for one-flesh companionship (Genesis 2:18-24 BSB), Paul teaches marriage’s role in guarding against sexual immorality and in mutual care (1 Corinthians 7:2-5 BSB), and Ephesians explains marriage as a covenantal picture of Christ and the church and a means of mutual sanctification (Ephesians 5:21-33 BSB). Scripture also affirms the blessing and responsibility of children and family life (Genesis 1:28 BSB), and warns that marriage must be entered with faithfulness and honor (Hebrews 13:4 BSB).

Practical, theological reasons to marry include:
- Covenant companionship and mutual care, not merely personal fulfillment (Genesis 2:18-24 BSB).  
- A context for sexual intimacy and faithfulness (1 Corinthians 7:2-5 BSB; Hebrews 13:4 BSB).  
- A means of sanctification, when spouses seek to serve and sharpen one another in Christ (Ephesians 5:21-33 BSB).  
- Being a living symbol of Christ’s love for the church (Ephesians 5:31-32 BSB).  
- Openness to children and raising them in covenant faith (Genesis 1:28 BSB).

A balanced caution: do not marry to fix loneliness, escape sin, or pursue selfish goals; motives matter (1 Corinthians 7:39 BSB). If you’re unsure, singleness can be a blessed season for devotion and focused service to the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:7-8, 32-35 BSB).

One practical next step: pray and ask God for clarity about his call, then talk with a trusted pastor or mature believer and pursue premarital counseling if you and your partner are considering marriage. Would you like help thinking through your specific situation or questions you’re facing about marriage?
Is Sunday the Christian Sabbath?

Yes. Christians have historically treated Sunday, the Lord's Day, as the Christian Sabbath in principle—a weekly day set apart for worship and rest—though its timing and form of observance differ from the Jewish seventh-day Sabbath.  

An opposing historic view (Seventh-day Sabbatarianism) holds that the Fourth Commandment still requires Sabbath observance on the seventh day (Saturday) and therefore Christians should keep Saturday as the Sabbath.  

My position (brief): I affirm Sunday as the Christian Sabbath, because the New Testament shows the first day was set apart in light of Christ’s resurrection (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2; Revelation 1:10 BSB), the Fourth Commandment’s moral principle of weekly rest and worship endures while its ceremonial sign is fulfilled in Christ (Exodus 20:8-11 BSB; Colossians 2:16-17 BSB; Hebrews 4:9-10 BSB), and Jesus is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:27-28 BSB).  

Practical application: honor the Lord’s Day by making corporate worship a priority, resting from nonessential work, and using time for family and personal spiritual renewal (for example, attend your church’s Sunday service and set aside at least one hour that day for Bible reading and prayer).
What does it mean that those who humble themselves will be exalted (Matthew 23:12)?

Jesus means that God honors and raises up those whose hearts are truly lowly before him—who renounce self-exaltation, repent of pride, and depend on Christ—while he resists the proud (Matthew 23:12).  

In context Jesus is rebuking the scribes and Pharisees for public, self-seeking glory, so his word is both a warning and a promise: God will, in his time, vindicate and lift up the humble and will oppose those who seek human praise (Matthew 23:1-12; Luke 18:14). This exaltation has two related senses: a present, relational blessing (vindication, usefulness, God’s favor in the life of the believer as they are sanctified) and an ultimate, eschatological glorification when Christ returns and the humble are vindicated and glorified (Philippians 2:5-11; 1 Peter 5:6). See also Luke 14:11; James 4:10 for the same promise to the lowly.

Theologically, Jesus speaks to sanctification (the Spirit-formed humility that shapes Christian life) and to God’s final exaltation (glorification) of those who trust Christ. Humility here is not false modesty but repentant dependence and willing service modeled supremely by Christ in his humiliation and subsequent exaltation (Philippians 2:5-11).

A practical next step: ask God to show you one area of pride, confess it briefly each morning, and do one concrete, unnoticed act of service this week; invite a mature believer or an elder to pray with you about it. If you’d like, I can suggest a short Scripture-reading plan (for example Philippians 2; Matthew 23; James 4) and a few simple exercises to help grow humility.
How should poetry in the Bible be interpreted?

Short answer: Read biblical poetry as a distinct literary genre that uses parallelism, metaphor, vivid imagery, and concentrated language to teach theological truth, move the heart, and form worship, and interpret it by careful exegesis rather than by forcing literal or purely allegorical readings.

Why and how, in practice:
- Recognize the genre first, Psalms, Lamentations, much of Job, Proverbs, Song of Songs, and many prophetic sections are poetry, not prose, so their meaning is shaped by figurative language and rhetorical patterning.
- Attend to literary features such as parallelism, repetition, chiasm, acrostics, metaphor and simile, and emotional tone, because Hebrew poetry often makes its point by balance and image rather than propositional argument.
- Read the immediate and wider context, the historical situation, and the canonical context, because poetry speaks within a storyline and covenant reality; let the New Testament’s use of the Old inform interpretation rather than replace careful exegesis, since Christians read the whole Bible as inspired (see 2 Timothy 3:16-17).
- Expect figurative language to convey real theological claims, for example shepherd metaphors teach about God’s care, not merely sentimental images, but avoid unrestrained allegorizing that imposes meanings the author did not intend.
- Let Scripture interpret Scripture where appropriate, because the New Testament often reads Old Testament poetry christologically (compare Psalm 110:1 with Hebrews 1:5-6; likewise Psalm 22:1 in Matthew 27:46; see also Luke 20:42-44).
- Ask the canonical and Christocentric question, how does this passage point to God’s character, Christ’s person and work, or the gospel? Use theological categories like typology, covenant, justification, and sanctification to connect text to doctrine without inventing novel doctrines.

Concrete application (one simple next step):
- Choose a psalm, identify its type (praise, lament, thanksgiving, royal), underline the key metaphors, ask “What does this teach me about God and my need?” then look for New Testament cross-references and pray the psalm back to God, letting it shape your worship and trust.

Helpful passages for these convictions:
- On Scripture’s authority and usefulness, 2 Timothy 3:16-17; 
- On the New Testament’s use of Old Testament poetry, Hebrews 1:5-6; Luke 20:42-44; Matthew 27:46; Psalm 110:1; Psalm 22:1.

If you like, give me a specific psalm or poetic passage and I will walk through it line by line, pointing out parallelism, figures of speech, possible New Testament connections, and a short application for your life.
What is the significance of the statement in Romans 1:22 that “professing themselves to be wise, they became fools”?

Briefly: Paul means that people who boast in human wisdom while rejecting God's revelation end up truly foolish, because by suppressing the truth about God they invert reality—exchanging the Creator’s glory for idols and thereby corrupting their thinking and life (Romans 1:20–23; Romans 1:22).

Why this matters theologically and biblically:
- Paul is diagnosing human depravity: although God has made himself known in creation (natural revelation), people suppress that truth and, in pride, trust their own reason or culture instead, which results in futile thinking and a darkened heart (Romans 1:20–21; Romans 1:28).
- The “wise” versus “fool” contrast exposes idolatry as not merely mistaken religion but a cognitive and moral collapse—what looks like wisdom apart from God becomes moral and intellectual folly (Romans 1:23; Romans 1:22).
- This passage explains why God’s righteous judgment (his “giving them over”) follows idolatry: false worship leads to sinful passions and actions (Romans 1:24–27; Romans 1:28).
- The point also echoes New Testament teaching that God’s true wisdom often appears foolish to worldly wisdom (see 1 Corinthians 1:18–25), showing the necessity of humility before God and faith in Christ.

A short historical-linguistic note: Paul wrote into a Greco-Roman culture that prized philosophical wisdom, so his charge stings—those who thought themselves “wise” (Greek sophos) actually proved “foolish” (Greek moros) when they exchanged revealed truth for speculation or idols.

Practical next step: Read Romans 1:18–23 slowly in the Berean Standard Bible, ask God to show any places you trust human reasoning or cultural approval more than him, confess those areas, and replace them by reading and submitting to Scripture (for example Romans 1:16–17; 1 Corinthians 1:18–25). If you’d like, I can walk through the passage with you verse by verse.
What does it mean that sin is lawlessness?

Briefly: 1 John 3:4 says “sin is lawlessness” to teach that sin is fundamentally rebellion against God’s righteous law and authority, not merely breaking a human rule (1 John 3:4 BSB).

A few clarifying points:
- The Greek behind the phrase, anomia, literally means “without law,” so John is calling sin lawlessness in the sense of refusing or overturning God’s moral order. This is more than isolated acts; it describes a willful posture of disobedience. (1 John 3:4 BSB)
- Scripture treats God’s law as an expression of his holy character (for example, the commands summed up in love for God and neighbor), so to live in lawlessness is to reject the covenantal, moral rule God has given for human flourishing (Matthew 22:37-40 BSB).
- The problem is universal and rooted in the heart: all persons fall short and are prone to this rebellion (Romans 3:23 BSB; Jeremiah 17:9 BSB).

Theological and pastoral significance:
- Legally and morally, lawlessness exposes our guilt before God and the earned consequence of sin — death — but the gospel gives the remedy: Christ bore the penalty in our place (penal substitution), died and rose to justify sinners who repent and trust him (Romans 6:23 BSB; Romans 5:8 BSB; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 BSB). Jesus himself came to undo the works of the one who holds people in sin (1 John 3:8 BSB).
- Practical application: recognizing that sin is lawlessness turns the Christian response away from moralism or casual compromise and toward humble repentance, dependence on Christ, and obedience formed by the Spirit (justification by faith leads to progressive sanctification).

One concrete next step: if you’re wrestling with this personally, read 1 John 1:8-10 and confess specific sins to God, asking for forgiveness and the Spirit’s power to change; also speak with a faithful elder or mature believer for accountability and prayer (1 John 1:8-10 BSB).

If you’d like, I can walk through 1 John 3 and 1 John 1 with you verse by verse or suggest a short confessional prayer outline to help you repent and trust Christ. Would you like that?
Has anyone ever seen God?

Short answer: Both yes and no. No one has ever seen the invisible, uncreated essence of God; yet God has truly been revealed and seen in history—most supremely in the incarnate Son, Jesus Christ.

No one has seen God’s invisible essence, as Scripture says, “No one has ever seen God” (John 1:18 BSB) and that God is “dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no one has seen or can see” (1 Timothy 6:16 BSB). At the same time the Bible records encounters with the living God in visible, mediated ways: Moses spoke with God “face to face, as a man speaks with his friend,” though God told Moses, “you cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live,” and Moses was allowed to see God’s back (Exodus 33:11 BSB; Exodus 33:18-23 BSB). Jacob also said, “I have seen God face to face” after wrestling at Peniel (Genesis 32:30 BSB), and prophets had visions of the Lord (for example, Isaiah’s vision, Isaiah 6:1-5 BSB).

How are these reconciled? Christians hold that God has uniquely and finally revealed himself in the Incarnation: the eternal Son of God became man so we might behold God. “The Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14 BSB), and Jesus is “the image of the invisible God” and “the radiance of his glory, the exact representation of his nature” (Colossians 1:15 BSB; Hebrews 1:3 BSB). Jesus himself said, “Whoever has seen me has seen the Father” (John 14:9 BSB). This connects to core doctrines—Trinity and the Incarnation (the hypostatic union)—that teach God is one in three persons and that the Son, without ceasing to be God, took on human nature so God could be truly seen.

Hopeful future note: God’s people will see him fully in the age to come—“they will see his face” (Revelation 22:4 BSB).

A concrete next step: if you want to “see” God as Scripture invites, read the Gospel of John (start with John 1 and John 14) and ask the Lord to reveal himself to you in Christ; if you’re exploring faith and want help, tell me whether you’re curious or searching and I’ll gently point you to the way of repentance and trust in Jesus.
Are books such as 90 minutes in Heaven and 23 minutes in Hell biblically based?

Short answer: No—those books are personal near-death/after-death testimonies, not new, binding revelation, and they should not be treated as fully “biblical” in the sense of equal authority with Scripture. They sometimes agree with biblical truths but also go beyond Scripture in sensational detail and occasionally conflict with how the Bible describes the afterlife and judgment.

Why I say that, briefly:
- The Bible is our final authority for doctrine, not private visions (the principle of sola Scriptura). Test what you read against Scripture, because the Bible alone is infallible (2 Corinthians 5:8; Philippians 1:23; John 14:1-3).
- Scripture does teach an intermediate presence with the Lord for believers and a real future judgment: believers “away from the body and at home with the Lord” (2 Corinthians 5:8), Paul’s longing “to depart and be with Christ” (Philippians 1:23), Jesus’ words about a prepared place (John 14:1-3), and the reality of judgment and eternal destinies (Luke 16:19–31; Matthew 25:31–46; Revelation 20:11–15).
- At the same time, many near-death accounts include precise sensory details, timetables, or theological claims that Scripture does not record. The Bible gives important truths about heaven and hell but not the kind of exhaustive, highly specific sightseeing reports these books sometimes offer.
- Scripture also warns us to test spiritual reports: “Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God” (1 John 4:1 BSB). That means approving what matches Scripture and rejecting or questioning what does not.

Practical next step you can take:
- Read the key biblical passages (John 14:1–3; 2 Corinthians 5:8; Philippians 1:23; Luke 16:19–31; Matthew 25:31–46; Revelation 20:11–15) and note where a book’s claims agree or disagree. Discuss any concerns with a trusted pastor or elder so you don’t form doctrine from a single testimony.

If you’d like, tell me a specific claim from 90 Minutes in Heaven or 23 Minutes in Hell and I’ll help compare it to what Scripture teaches.
Why does God not seem to care about the millions of starving children in the world?

I hear the pain and anger in your question — it’s heartbreaking to watch children starve and to feel as if God is silent. That makes sense, and your question is honest and heavy.

Short answer: God does care deeply about the poor and the vulnerable, but we live in a fallen world where human sin, broken systems, and evil bring terrible suffering; God is present in suffering and also calls his people to act. Psalm 68:5 says God is “a father of the fatherless and a defender of widows” and James 1:27 calls true religion to “visit orphans and widows in their affliction” (Psalm 68:5; James 1:27 BSB). Jesus himself showed compassion for the hungry and suffering and healed the sick (Matthew 9:36; Matthew 14:14 BSB).

Why suffering still exists
- The Fall and human sin distorted creation, so injustice, selfishness, corrupt systems, and greed cause much of the famine and neglect we see. Genesis records the world’s brokenness after sin entered.
- God allows human freedom and often works through people (or the lack of restraint on people) — so much suffering is the result of human choices and failed institutions.
- God is not absent in suffering; he is near the brokenhearted and sympathizes with us in our weakness (Psalm 34:18; Hebrews 4:15–16 BSB). He can bring good even through evil, as Joseph said to his brothers, “You meant me harm, but God meant it for good” (Genesis 50:20; Romans 8:28 BSB).

What God asks of his people
The Bible calls Christians to compassion, justice, and tangible care: “Learn to do good, seek justice, correct oppression, defend the fatherless” (Isaiah 1:17 BSB). That means God often answers suffering by calling his people to intervene, give, pray, and advocate (James 1:27; Isaiah 1:17 BSB).

Practical next steps you can take right now
- Pray specifically for those children and for wise, God-fearing leaders to act (God hears and is near; Psalm 34:18; Hebrews 4:16 BSB).  
- Give or partner with trustworthy relief organizations (for example, Christian ministries such as Samaritan’s Purse, World Vision, or Compassion International, or your local church’s mercy ministries), or contact your church about sponsored relief projects.  
- Advocate and raise awareness in your community or church for policies and programs that prevent famine and protect children.

A brief Gospel reminder
God is holy and we are sinners in need of a Savior; Jesus lived, died in our place, and rose again to make forgiveness and new life possible. Trusting Christ — turning from sin and relying on Jesus alone — is the way to be reconciled to God (Romans 3 and 4; 1 Corinthians 15 BSB). If you want to talk about that or need help finding a local church to join in practical help, I’ll gladly help.

If you’d like, tell me whether you want prayer suggestions, help evaluating charities, or concrete ways to get your church involved — I can walk through those next steps with you.
What is postmodern therapy, and is it biblical?

Short definition: Postmodern therapy describes several counseling approaches influenced by postmodern philosophy that emphasize clients’ stories, social construction of meaning, relational context, and collaborative, non-expert therapist roles. Common examples are narrative therapy, solution-focused brief therapy, and social constructionist approaches.

Is it biblical? Brief answer: Some techniques that come from postmodern therapy can be helpful tools for listening, drawing out a person’s story, and encouraging hope, but the underlying postmodern worldview—radical relativism about truth, skepticism of objective moral and spiritual reality, and the sidelining of authoritative truth—conflicts with core biblical commitments (for example, Scripture’s authority and the reality of sin and objective gospel truth). 2 Timothy 3:16-17 supports Scripture’s authority; Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23 show our need for gospel change; John 14:6 affirms Christ’s unique role as Savior; Hebrews 4:12 reminds us the Word judges the heart.

Two major Christian responses, in one sentence each:
- Integrationists: Christian counselors may use postmodern techniques as neutral methods, testing and shaping them under Scripture so the Gospel and biblical anthropology (sin, repentance, sanctification) remain central.  
- Reformers (biblical counseling proponents): Reject postmodern epistemology and some of its methods when they undermine objective truth, preferring counseling explicitly built on Scripture and pastoral authority.

My position and why, in one sentence plus brief reason: I favor cautious, gospel-centered use of helpful postmodern techniques when they are submitted to sola Scriptura and incorporated within a counseling plan that explains sin, Christ’s substitutionary atonement, and progressive sanctification, because methods are tools but the Bible alone defines our goals and means of change. Theological concerns include preserving penal substitutionary atonement, justification by faith, and the church’s role in shepherding, not replacing those with therapeutic autonomy.

Practical application and next step:
- If you are seeking therapy, ask potential counselors how they view human nature, sin, Scripture, and the goal of counseling; prefer a counselor who will cooperate with your pastor or elders and who will name the need for gospel change, not only symptom relief.  
- If you are a counselor, test every technique against Scripture, prioritize repentance and faith as ends, and use methods that foster confession, community, and discipleship (James 5:16; 2 Timothy 3:16-17).

If your heart is weighed by guilt or you wonder about forgiveness, remember the gospel: all have sinned and fall short, Christ died and rose to forgive and give new life, and God calls you to repent and trust in Jesus alone for salvation (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; John 14:6). Would you like help formulating questions to ask a counselor or a short checklist to evaluate whether a therapist is gospel-centered?
I am a Christian who is addicted to smoking. How can I quit?

Thank you for being honest — quitting a smoking addiction is painful and hard, and I’m glad you reached out. The Bible calls us to care for our bodies and to fight slavery to sin, and God offers forgiveness and power to change. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20; Romans 6:6-7.

A short gospel reminder first: if you have sinned by allowing this habit to enslave you, confess it and receive God’s forgiveness; Christ’s death and resurrection free sinners and empower us for new life. 1 John 1:9; Romans 6:4. Trusting Jesus and walking by the Spirit is the foundation of lasting change (sanctification). Galatians 5:16.

Practical, biblical steps you can take now
1) Make a clear quit plan and a date. Pick a quit day within the next 7 days, remove cigarettes and lighters from your home and car, and identify your main triggers (stress, driving, social situations). 1 Corinthians 10:13.  
2) Tell accountable Christians and ask for prayer and checks. Name two trusted believers (a pastor, elder, or mature friend) and ask them to pray and to call or meet weekly. Confession and mutual prayer are powerful. James 5:16.  
3) Replace the habit with healthier rhythms. When the urge hits, have immediate alternatives: a short walk, prayer, Scripture (memorized verse), water, or a breathing exercise. Practice a new simple habit tied to triggers (for example, after each meal, pray one sentence or read a verse). Galatians 5:16.  
4) Rely on the Spirit and God’s Word daily. Pray honestly about cravings, read a few short verses each morning and when tempted, and confess failures quickly rather than hiding them. Philippians 4:6-7; 2 Corinthians 12:9.  
5) Get practical medical and counseling help. I cannot give medical advice, but many people benefit from nicotine replacement, medication, behavioral therapy, or staffed quit programs — consult your doctor or a qualified counselor about options that fit you. If you prefer Christian counseling, consider a biblical counselor or your church elders.  
6) Plan for setbacks and keep going. Slips can happen; confess, learn, and keep pursuing victory by God’s grace — God is patient and keeps working in you until the day of Christ. 1 John 1:9; Philippians 1:6.

One immediate next step I recommend
- Choose a quit date within the next 7 days, tell two Christian friends and ask them to pray and check in weekly. James 5:16; Philippians 4:6-7.

A few encouraging truths to hold on to
- You are not fighting this alone; the Spirit helps you and Christ’s grace sustains you. Galatians 5:16; 2 Corinthians 12:9.  
- God cares about your body and your freedom from slavery to anything that controls you. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20; Romans 6:6-7.  
- There is hope: “No temptation has overtaken you except what is common to mankind, and God is faithful to provide a way out.” 1 Corinthians 10:13.

If you’d like, I can:
- Help you set a specific 30-day quit plan with daily micro-goals, or  
- Draft a short accountability message you can send to two friends, or  
- Suggest Bible verses to memorize for cravings.

What is mortification of sin / the flesh?

Mortification of sin (the flesh) is the Spirit-wrought putting to death, day by day, of sinful desires, habits, and actions in the life of a believer; it is a vital part of progressive sanctification, not a way to earn justification. Romans 8:13; Colossians 3:5-10.

Why the Bible calls for it, and how it happens
- Scripture commands believers to "put to death" the deeds of the body and to walk by the Spirit so sin will not control us, showing that mortification is both commanded and enabled by the Holy Spirit, not self-help alone (Romans 8:13; Galatians 5:16-17).  
- The work is connected to our union with Christ (we died with him and are to reckon ourselves dead to sin), and to putting on the new self formed in righteousness, which shows the theological link to justification and sanctification (Romans 6:6-7; Ephesians 4:22-24).  
- We cooperate with God’s grace in this: we are to "work out your salvation" even as God works in us to will and to do his good pleasure (Philippians 2:12-13).

Concrete, practical steps you can take (one small plan to begin)
1) Name and confess a specific besetting sin to God and a trusted believer (1 John 1:9), then ask that person for regular accountability.  
2) Replace the sin habit with Scripture and prayer—memorize one verse that opposes the temptation and pray it when tempted (Psalm 119:11; Galatians 5:16).  
3) Remove or limit triggers (people, media, places) that feed the flesh.  
4) Use spiritual disciplines: daily Bible reading, brief focused prayer, and occasional fasting to strengthen dependence on the Spirit.  
5) Participate in a local church where God’s Word and the means of grace (preaching, fellowship, the Lord’s Supper) shape you.

A pastoral encouragement
This is God's work in you—he gives the Spirit and the power to mortify sin—so do not despair when progress is slow; persevere in confession and the means of grace (Romans 6:11-14; Philippians 1:6). If you’d like, tell me one specific struggle and I’ll suggest a two-week plan tailored to it.
Is God’s love reckless?

Short answer: No, God’s love is not reckless. It is lavish and willing to save sinners, yet it is also holy, just, and wise.

God’s love is shown in coming to a guilty world and bearing our sin in Christ, which is costly and intentional, not careless, John 3:16; Romans 5:8; 1 John 4:9-10. Scripture emphasizes God’s mercy and initiative in seeking the lost, Ephesians 2:4-5; Luke 15:4-7, so his love pursues sinners even when they are undeserving.

At the same time, God is holy and just, and sin has consequences, Romans 1:18; Hebrews 12:6. The cross shows how God unites love and justice: Christ’s substitutionary suffering satisfied God’s righteous wrath while securing forgiveness for those who trust him, a clear demonstration of penal substitutionary atonement in Scripture, Romans 3:25. That is why God’s love is powerful and wise, not reckless.

Practical step: read John 3:16, Romans 5:8, and Romans 3:25 this week, pray asking God to show you the cross afresh, and if you’d like, I can help you think through what trusting Christ looks like or suggest a local Bible-believing church to talk with. Would you like that?
Does the Bible teach that there is a gift of celibacy?

Short answer: Yes, the New Testament presents celibacy or singleness as a God‑given gift or charism for some people.  Paul explicitly calls it a gift, and Jesus affirms voluntary singleness for the sake of the kingdom (1 Corinthians 7:7; 1 Corinthians 7:8–9; Matthew 19:11–12).

One opposing view in one sentence: Some Christians say celibacy is not a distinct supernatural “gift” but simply a wise or providential calling, a discipline, or a practical estate chosen for ministry rather than a charism given by God.

My position and why, briefly: I affirm that Scripture treats singleness/celibacy as a legitimate gift and calling from God, intended to allow undivided devotion to the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:32–35), yet it is not morally superior to marriage and is not required of all (1 Corinthians 7:7–9). Paul’s counsel shows both states (marriage and singleness) are honorable and God‑ordained, with singleness sometimes being a special enabling for kingdom service (1 Corinthians 7:7; 1 Corinthians 7:32–35; Matthew 19:11–12).

Practical next step: If you are considering celibacy as a calling, pray for clarity, study 1 Corinthians 7 and Matthew 19:11–12, and speak with your pastor or a mature elder to test your motives and to arrange accountability and a ministry plan suited to singleness. Would you like help walking through those passages together or drafting some questions to bring to your pastor?
What did it mean that Satan wanted to be like the Most High in Isaiah 14:14?

Brief answer: In Isaiah 14:14 the claim "I will be like the Most High" expresses arrogant self-exaltation, the desire to usurp God’s unique sovereignty and honor rather than submit to him, a pride that leads to a catastrophic fall (Isaiah 14:12-15 BSB).

Two major ways Christians read the passage:
- One historic reading sees these verses as picturing Satan's prideful attempt to exalt himself equal with God, an event referenced in the New Testament’s pictures of Satan’s fall (Luke 10:18 BSB; Revelation 12:7-9 BSB; 2 Corinthians 11:14 BSB).
- Another reading, more strictly literary-historical, understands the oracle as primarily a taunt against the human king of Babylon boasting to set his throne above God, a clear example of imperial pride addressed in Isaiah’s context (Isaiah 14:4-6 BSB; Isaiah 14:12-15 BSB).

My judgment in one sentence: The best reading holds both: Isaiah’s immediate target is the proud king of Babylon, but the prophet uses cosmic, poetic language that also pictures the same sin at the spiritual level—Satanic pride and attempted usurpation of God—so the line "I will be like the Most High" names the same underlying sin whether spoken by a human tyrant or the rebel angel (compare Ezekiel 28:12-19 BSB).

Theological point: The verse exposes the sin of pride and usurpation, theological terms include pride, rebellion, and the denial of God’s sovereignty, and it illustrates the biblical teaching that self-exaltation precedes divine judgment (Proverbs 16:18 BSB; James 4:6 BSB).

Practical application: Ask God to show you places of pride in your heart, confess them, and practice humility by meditating on Christ’s humility and exaltation (Philippians 2:5-11 BSB); a concrete next step is to read Isaiah 14:12-15 BSB and Philippians 2:5-11 BSB this week and pray, "Search me, O God" as you confess any impulse to be your own lord (Psalm 139:23-24 BSB).
What does the Bible say about the hornet?

Briefly: in the Bible the hornet (a stinging insect) is mainly presented as an instrument of God’s judgment and of His providential help in driving out Israel’s enemies, not as a symbolic virtue in itself. This teaches God’s sovereign rule over creation and His power to deliver His people by unexpected means, a truth tied to divine providence and judgment.

Key passages:
- God promises to send hornets to clear the land for Israel, as part of His conquest plan (Exodus 23:28–30).  
- The same promise is repeated as a means God will use to make the Canaanites flee or perish before Israel (Deuteronomy 7:20; Deuteronomy 11:25).  
- God reminds Israel that He sent a “hornet” to drive nations out when He delivered them (Joshua 24:12).

A short historical-linguistic note: the Hebrew words translated “hornet” (and related LXX renderings) most likely refer to stinging wasp-type insects; translators differ about exact species, but the theological point is clear—the animal world is used by God as an instrument of His will.

Practical application: when you feel overwhelmed by obstacles, remember God can remove impediments in ways you do not expect; read Exodus 23:28–30 and Deuteronomy 7:20, pray asking God to act by His providence, and tell a trusted brother or sister in Christ to pray with you. If you want, I can quote those passages in the Berean Standard Bible and help you work through them devotionally.
What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?

Brief answer: The key events in Jesus Christ’s life move from the incarnation and virgin birth through his public ministry, atoning death, bodily resurrection, and ascension—events that accomplish the incarnation, atonement (penal substitution), and resurrection that secure salvation by faith. These are recorded in the four Gospels and summarized theologically as the work of the eternal Son who is fully God and fully man (hypostatic union).

Chronological highlights (each line gives a short description and primary Gospel references):
- Conception and annunciation, Mary told she will bear the Son of God (incarnation, virgin birth), fulfilling prophecy, Mary’s song. Luke 1:26–38; Matthew 1:18–25.  
- Birth in Bethlehem, shepherds and Magi, Simeon and Anna’s recognition. Luke 2:1–20; Matthew 2:1–12; Luke 2:22–40.  
- Flight to Egypt and return to Nazareth, childhood in Nazareth (early fulfillment of prophecy). Matthew 2:13–23; Luke 2:41–52.  
- Baptism by John and divine affirmation, marking the start of public ministry. Matthew 3:13–17; Mark 1:9–11; Luke 3:21–22.  
- Wilderness temptation, demonstrating sinless obedience. Matthew 4:1–11; Luke 4:1–13.  
- Calling of the Twelve, teaching (for example Sermon on the Mount), parables, and many miracles—signs of the kingdom of God and authority of Christ. Matthew 4:18–22; Matthew 5–7; Mark 1:21–34; Luke 8:1–3; John 2:1–11.  
- Transfiguration, confirming his divine glory to select disciples. Matthew 17:1–9; Mark 9:2–8; Luke 9:28–36.  
- Triumphal entry into Jerusalem, cleansing the temple, escalating conflict with religious leaders. Matthew 21:1–11; John 12:12–19; Matthew 21:12–17.  
- The Last Supper, institution of the Lord’s Supper, and farewell teaching (new covenant promise). Matthew 26:17–29; Luke 22:7–20; John 13:1–17.  
- Betrayal, arrest, trials before Jewish and Roman authorities, Peter’s denial. Matthew 26:47–75; John 18:1–27.  
- Crucifixion, death, and burial, the atoning sacrifice for sin (penal substitution and propitiation). Matthew 27:32–61; Mark 15:21–47; Luke 23:26–56; John 19:16–42.  
- Bodily resurrection on the third day, appearances to many, the decisive victory over sin and death. Matthew 28:1–10; Luke 24:1–49; John 20:1–21:25; 1 Corinthians 15:3–8.  
- Ascension to the Father and promise of the Holy Spirit, commissioning of the church (Great Commission). Luke 24:50–53; Acts 1:6–11; Matthew 28:18–20.

Theological significance in brief: Jesus’ life shows the incarnation (God becoming man), his sinless life and fulfillment of the Law, substitutionary atonement on the cross (he bore our sin), and his bodily resurrection vindicating his victory and granting justification by faith (Romans 5:1), with his ascension and promise to return (Acts 1:11; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Isaiah 53; 1 Peter 2:24).

Gospel reminder and invitation: The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory, we cannot save ourselves, and Christ died and rose for our sins so that whoever repents and trusts in him receives forgiveness and new life (Romans 3:23; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Isaiah 53; 1 Peter 2:24; Romans 5:1). Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

One practical next step: Read the key Gospel passages in order—Luke 1–2 (incarnation and birth), John 1 (who Jesus is), Matthew 4 and Luke 4 (start of ministry and temptation), John 6 or Matthew 5–7 (teaching), and John 20 (resurrection). Join a Bible-teaching local church to grow under pastoral care.
