What is the hypostatic union?

The hypostatic union is the Christian doctrine that in the one person (hypostasis) of Jesus Christ two complete natures—true God and true man—are united, so that these natures are joined without mixture, confusion, change, division, or separation. John 1:14; Colossians 2:9.

Why it matters: this explains how Jesus can both perfectly reveal and represent God to us and also truly suffer, die, and rise as a human being—only a person who is both God and man could be our perfect mediator and atoning Savior. Philippians 2:6-8; Hebrews 2:14-17; 1 Timothy 3:16.

Pastoral application: because Christ is fully God and fully man, we may trust him to sympathize with our weaknesses and to save us completely, so we can approach God confidently through him. Hebrews 4:14-16. Practical next step: read John 1 and meditate on John 1:14 and Philippians 2:6-8 this week, asking God to deepen your trust in Jesus.

Brief Gospel reminder: all have sinned and need a Savior, Christ died and was raised for our sins, therefore repent and trust Jesus alone for forgiveness and new life. Romans 3:23; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Would you like a short 7‑day reading plan focused on the person of Christ?
Doubting God — how can I overcome doubt in my relationship with God?

First, you are not alone—doubt is a common part of the Christian life and can even deepen your faith when handled biblically. Christians in Scripture struggled with doubt (for example, Thomas; John 20:27-29 BSB) and Jesus met them there with mercy and truth (for example, Peter; Matthew 14:31 BSB). 

What the Bible teaches, briefly: faith is trust in God’s promises, not the absence of questions (Hebrews 11:1 BSB). The Holy Spirit witnesses to our standing in Christ and secures our assurance (Romans 8:16 BSB; Ephesians 1:13-14 BSB). God is committed to finishing the work of sanctification he began in us (Philippians 1:6 BSB).

Practical, pastoral steps to begin overcoming doubt
- Bring the doubt to God honestly in prayer. Jesus welcomed honest questions; don’t hide yours. Ask God for faith (Mark 9:24 BSB).  
- Test doubts against Scripture, not feelings. Memorize and meditate on promises about God’s character and love (for example, Romans 8:38-39 BSB; Psalm 77:11-12 BSB). Read Gospel accounts of Jesus’ life, death, and resurrection, which ground our hope.  
- Name the source: are your doubts intellectual (questions), emotional (pain, grief), moral (unrepentant sin), or spiritual attack? Each needs a different response—study, lament, confession, or spiritual warfare with means of grace.  
- Confess doubt and seek help in Christian community. Tell a trusted elder, pastor, or mature believer and ask them to pray and point you to Scripture (Galatians 6:2 implied).  
- Take small faith-steps. Obedience often strengthens trust: serve, worship, give, or read Scripture even when you don’t “feel” it. Faith grows through means of grace (Word, prayer, sacraments, fellowship).  
- Keep a “faith journal.” Record specific ways God has proven faithful in the past—this helps recall God’s deeds when doubts rise (Psalm 77:11-12 BSB).  
- If doubt is bound up with deep depression, trauma, or prolonged spiritual crisis, seek professional help alongside pastoral care (a Christian counselor or your church elders).

A short, concrete next step for this week
- Pick one promise to memorize (for example, Romans 8:38-39 BSB or Ephesians 1:13-14 BSB). Each morning this week read that passage aloud, pray one honest sentence about your doubt, and write one line of how you saw God during the day. Then share your journal entry with a trusted Christian friend or pastor.

If you want, tell me whether your doubt feels mainly intellectual (questions about truth), emotional (hurt, disappointment), or spiritual (assurance of salvation). I can give a short Scripture plan and a few focused resources for that specific kind of doubt.
Did God create other people in addition to Adam and Eve?

Short answer: The Bible presents Adam and Eve as the first human pair and teaches that all people descend from them, not that God separately created other unrelated human beings. Genesis 1:27; Genesis 2:7; Genesis 3; Genesis 4:1, Genesis 4:16–17; Genesis 5:4; 1 Corinthians 15:45–49; Romans 5:12–19.

One-sentence statement of the opposing view: Some propose polygenism—the idea God created multiple original human pairs—arguing Genesis does not name every person (for example, Cain’s wife) and that other people could have existed alongside Adam and Eve.

My position and why: I hold to biblical monogenism (Adam and Eve as the sole human progenitors) because Scripture consistently treats Adam as the “first man” and the federal head whose fall brings sin and death to all (1 Corinthians 15:45–49; Romans 5:12–19), and Genesis explicitly describes their children and the spreading of humanity (Genesis 4–5). Theologically this preserves the biblical teaching of original sin and the need for Christ’s one atoning work as the remedy for Adam’s fall.

A few practical notes and next steps: if you’re puzzled by passages like “Cain’s wife,” remember Genesis 5:4 says Adam “fathered other sons and daughters,” which explains early marriages; read Genesis 1–5 and Romans 5 slowly in prayer, ask your pastor or a mature Bible-teacher to walk through the texts with you, and bring specific questions back here if you like.
What does the Bible say about self-discipline?

Briefly: the Bible teaches that self-discipline (self-control) is essential to Christian life—it is both commanded as part of holiness and a fruit God produces in us as we are sanctified. It is not mere stoic effort but grace-empowered obedience lived out by means of the Word, prayer, and fellowship.

Why and how Scripture frames it:
- Self-discipline is a fruit of the Spirit, showing the Spirit’s work in our lives (Galatians 5:22-23 BSB).  
- Paul models and commands disciplined effort in the Christian life, comparing it to athletic training and saying he disciplines his body (1 Corinthians 9:24-27 BSB).  
- God’s grace trains us to live self-controlled and godly, so discipline is part of our progressive sanctification (Titus 2:11-12 BSB; Hebrews 12:11 BSB).  
- The Spirit equips us; God does not give a spirit of timidity but of power, love, and self-discipline (2 Timothy 1:7 BSB).  
- Wisdom literature warns of the cost of lacking self-control (Proverbs 16:32 BSB) and calls for ruling one’s spirit.

Theological terms: sanctification, mortification (putting sin to death), fruit of the Spirit, and obedience are the biblical categories for understanding self-discipline.

One concrete application (next step): choose one specific area to practice self-discipline this week (for example, 10 minutes of Scripture and prayer each morning or fasting one meal before prayer), tell one trusted believer your plan and ask them to check in midweek, and use a short daily reminder to confess slip-ups and rely on the Spirit rather than only willpower.

Pastoral encouragement: this is a grace-shaped process—expect growth, setbacks, and God’s patient correction as you run the race and fix your eyes on Jesus (Hebrews 12:1-2 BSB). If you’d like, tell me one area you struggle with and I’ll suggest a focused plan and a verse to pray.
What is original sin?

Original sin is the biblical teaching that because of Adam’s disobedience the whole human race was born into a corrupted, sinful condition—so we inherit a sinful nature and are naturally hostile to God and unable to save ourselves apart from his grace. Genesis records the Fall (Genesis 3:1-7), and the New Testament teaches that sin and death spread to all people through Adam (Romans 5:12-19), that all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory (Romans 3:23), that we are by nature dead in trespasses (Ephesians 2:1-3), and that even David felt his sinfulness from birth (Psalm 51:5).

The doctrine has two closely related emphases in historic theology: inherited corruption (our bent toward sin, often summarized as total depravity) and, in classical Reformed formulation, the imputation of Adam’s guilt through federal headship (that Adam’s breach affected his whole posterity). Historically Christians have disagreed: one historic Reformed view teaches both inherited corruption and imputed guilt; Eastern Orthodox theology tends to speak of ancestral sin as inherited mortality and propensity to sin rather than juridical guilt imputed; Pelagianism denies any inherited corruption and asserts that humans are born morally neutral. My position, consistent with the historic Reformed and Reformed Baptist understanding, affirms that Scripture presents both universal corruption and the federal consequences of Adam’s sin, which is why we urgently need Christ (Romans 5:18-19).

Because original sin leaves us unable to reconcile ourselves to God, the gospel is the only hope: Jesus Christ lived the life we could not live, died as our substitute, was buried, and rose bodily to secure forgiveness and new life for those who believe (Romans 5:18-19). Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

If you’d like a next step, read Romans 5 and 6 slowly and ask God to show you where you need to repent, or talk with a pastor in a Bible-preaching church to walk through the gospel together. Would you like me to help you read Romans 5 now or suggest a short Scripture-based way to pray as you consider trusting Christ?
Are intrusive thoughts sin? Are spontaneous violent, sexual, or blasphemous thoughts sin?

Short answer: No—having an intrusive, spontaneous violent, sexual, or blasphemous thought is not automatically a willful sin. Sin occurs when you willingly consent to, cultivate, or take pleasure in those thoughts. Hebrews shows that Jesus understands temptation though he never sinned, which helps us see the difference between being tempted and actually sinning (Hebrews 4:15 BSB).

Why, biblically:
- Temptation and unwanted thoughts come from the world of temptation and our fallen desires, but desire must be consented to in order to become sin: Christians believe desire that is indulged leads to action, as James explains (James 1:14-15 BSB).  
- Jesus taught that when we willfully look with lust or allow anger to fester in the heart, that inner consenting is counted as sin (Matthew 5:27-28; Matthew 5:21-22 BSB).  
- Paul describes the Christian’s inward struggle with thoughts and impulses that do not always represent his will (Romans 7:15-25 BSB).  
- We are called to take thoughts captive to Christ and to reject those that oppose his rule (2 Corinthians 10:5 BSB).  
- When we repent and confess willful sin, God forgives (1 John 1:9 BSB), and there is no condemnation for those in Christ (Romans 8:1 BSB).

How to tell the difference in practice:
- If an intrusive thought shocks you, you resist it, and you do not dwell on it, that resistance is a good sign you have not sinned by it.  
- If you pause, imagine it in detail, rehearse it, or take pleasure in it, that is consenting and becomes sin (see Matthew 5:27-28; James 1:14-15 BSB).

Practical, pastoral steps you can take now:
1) Refuse and don’t rehearse the thought—practice immediately turning away and replacing it with truth (for example, meditate on Philippians 4:8; use 2 Corinthians 10:5 as a short prayer to take the thought captive) (Philippians 4:8; 2 Corinthians 10:5 BSB).  
2) Talk with a trusted pastor, elder, or a Christian counselor if the thoughts are persistent or cause great distress—persistent intrusive thoughts can be associated with anxiety or OCD and deserve wise help.  
3) Confess and repent whenever you discover you have consented to sinful imagination, and receive God’s forgiveness (1 John 1:9; Romans 8:1 BSB).  
4) Build habits that help: regular Scripture reading, prayer, accountability, and avoiding triggers that lead to willful dwelling (Psalm 139:23-24 BSB).

A word of encouragement: God knows your heart and meets you in your weakness; Christ sympathizes with our temptations and offers mercy and help (Hebrews 4:15-16; Psalm 139:23-24 BSB). If you’d like, tell me whether these thoughts are occasional or constant, and I can suggest a short Scripture-based practice and a next step for pastoral help.
Book of 1 Corinthians - Bible Survey

Short survey: 1 Corinthians is a pastoral letter from the Apostle Paul to the church in Corinth addressing serious moral failure, factional division, worship abuses, questions about marriage and food offered to idols, misuse of spiritual gifts, and doubts about the resurrection, calling the church to holiness, unity, and love under Christ’s headship. Authoritative themes include sanctification, ecclesiology, the Lord’s Supper, spiritual gifts, and the bodily resurrection.

Basic data
- Author and date: The Apostle Paul, written around AD 53–54 from Ephesus.  
- Recipient and context: The believers in the cosmopolitan city of Corinth, a mixed Jewish–Gentile church reported to Paul as divided and compromised (1 Corinthians 1:10; 1 Corinthians 5:1-2).  
- Occasion and purpose: Paul wrote in response to reports and a previous letter, aiming to correct sin, settle doctrinal and practical questions, defend his apostolic authority, and shape the church’s worship and witness (1 Corinthians 1:11; 1 Corinthians 7:1; 1 Corinthians 9:1-2).

Major themes with key passages
- Church unity and factionalism, call to unity in Christ, not loyalty to leaders, (1 Corinthians 1:10; 1 Corinthians 3:4-9).  
- Moral holiness and church discipline, confronting public immorality and calling for repentance (1 Corinthians 5:1-5; 1 Corinthians 6:18-20).  
- Marriage, singleness, and Christian freedom, practical pastoral counsel for everyday life (1 Corinthians 7:1-16; 1 Corinthians 8:1-13).  
- Food offered to idols and Christian liberty balanced by love for weaker brothers (1 Corinthians 8:4-13; 1 Corinthians 10:23-33).  
- Worship order and the Lord’s Supper, warning against irreverent participation and affirming Christ’s institution (1 Corinthians 11:23-26; 1 Corinthians 11:27-29).  
- Spiritual gifts and orderly worship, gifts are given for building up the body but must be governed by love and order (1 Corinthians 12:4-11; 1 Corinthians 13:1-3; 1 Corinthians 14:26-40).  
- The bodily resurrection, the gospel’s foundation and the hope of believers, culminating in the great treatment of resurrection theology (1 Corinthians 15:1-8; 1 Corinthians 15:20-22; 1 Corinthians 15:50-58).

Structure (brief)
- Chapters 1–4: divisions, wisdom, and servants of Christ.  
- Chapters 5–6: discipline and Christian conduct.  
- Chapter 7: marriage and singleness.  
- Chapters 8–10: liberty, conscience, and idolatry.  
- Chapters 11–14: worship, Lord’s Supper, head coverings, spiritual gifts, orderly worship.  
- Chapter 15: resurrection.  
- Chapter 16: final instructions and greetings.

Doctrinal significance (concise)
- Ecclesiology and discipline: teaches how the local church preserves holiness and witness through loving but firm correction (1 Corinthians 5:4-5; 1 Corinthians 6:1-7).  
- Sacramental practice: affirms Christ’s institution of the Lord’s Supper as a central means of remembering his death and assessing our hearts (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).  
- Eschatology and soteriology: grounds Christian hope in Christ’s historical death, burial, and bodily resurrection, which secures future resurrection for believers (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Corinthians 15:20-23).  
- Gifts and order: stresses that every gift serves the common good and must be exercised in love (1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Corinthians 13:1-3).

On spiritual gifts (secondary doctrine, brief per tradition)
- One historic Christian view, continuationism, holds that sign gifts such as tongues, prophecy, and miraculous healings continue today.  
- The other view, cessationism, holds that those sign gifts ceased with the apostolic era and that Scripture is now the normative revelation.  
- My position (Reformed Baptist, 1689 tradition): I hold cautious cessationism, emphasizing that the miraculous sign gifts were foundational to the apostolic age and that we should test claimed gifts by Scripture and sound fruit, because the church’s maturity rests on the completed apostolic witness and the ordinary means of grace.

Pastoral application (one concrete next step)
- Practical next step: This week read 1 Corinthians 12–14 and chapter 13 slowly, then ask God to show one specific way you can use your gifts to serve others in your local church; share that with one mature believer and ask them to pray and give accountability.  
- Additional concrete idea: memorize 1 Corinthians 13:1-3 as a corrective against pride in gifting and a daily reminder to pursue love.

Recommended reading plan (6 weeks)
- Week 1: 1 Corinthians 1–4, pray about unity (see 1 Corinthians 1:10).  
- Week 2: 1 Corinthians 5–7, examine personal holiness (see 1 Corinthians 6:19-20).  
- Week 3: 1 Corinthians 8–10, reflect on liberty and conscience (see 1 Corinthians 8:9-13).  
- Week 4: 1 Corinthians 11–12, study the Lord’s Supper and gifts (see 1 Corinthians 11:23-26; 1 Corinthians 12:7).  
- Week 5: 1 Corinthians 13–14, meditate on love and orderly worship (see 1 Corinthians 13:1-3).  
- Week 6: 1 Corinthians 15–16, anchor your hope in the resurrection (see 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Corinthians 15:58).

If you’d like, I can give a one-page outline you can print and use as a study guide, or a short devotional plan focusing on 1 Corinthians 13 and 15 for two weeks. Which would help you most?
How do I identify my spiritual gift?

A spiritual gift is a Spirit‑given ability (charismata) given to believers so the body of Christ is built up and God is glorified. See 1 Corinthians 12:4-11; Romans 12:6-8; Ephesians 4:11-13.

Practical steps to identify your gift
1. Pray and depend on the Spirit, asking God to reveal and empower you—remember the Spirit distributes gifts as He wills (1 Corinthians 12:11).
2. Learn what the Bible lists as gifts and their purpose so you know what to look for (Romans 12:6-8; 1 Corinthians 12:27-31; Ephesians 4:11-13; 1 Peter 4:10-11).
3. Serve where there is need, especially in your local church, and watch for consistent patterns: where you serve gladly, grow, and produce spiritual fruit (Galatians 5:22-23).
4. Seek affirmation from mature Christians and church leaders—the body’s recognition is an important confirmation (Ephesians 4:11-13).
5. Distinguish spiritual gifts from natural talents and from a vocational calling—gifts are for building others, talents may be repurposed for service, and calling is broader and long‑term.
6. Try multiple areas, be patient, and allow your gift to be refined in service and sanctification; gifts often become clearer in faithful practice (1 Corinthians 12:4-11).

A pastoral caution
- Let love govern everything; gifts without love are empty (1 Corinthians 13:1-3).  
- Use inventories and quizzes only as tools—never as final authority; the church and the Spirit’s fruit should confirm.

One concrete next step
This week, pick one ministry area your church needs (for example children’s ministry, hospitality, evangelism, teaching prep, mercy ministry) and serve consistently for four Sundays or weeks, ask your pastor or a mature believer for feedback, and daily ask God to show you how He is using you and to give you humility to receive correction (1 Corinthians 12:11; Ephesians 4:11-13).

If you want, tell me your church context, the kinds of service you enjoy, and any gifts people have already affirmed in you, and I’ll help you sort what that likely indicates.
What is lacking in Christ’s afflictions (Colossians 1:24)?

Brief answer: Paul is not saying Christ’s atoning sufferings were deficient in value or effect; rather he means that he (and the church) are completing, in experience and mission, the remaining sufferings of Christ’s body until the church’s calling is fulfilled (Colossians 1:24 BSB).

Two historic readings in one sentence each:
- Some read the phrase to imply a real lack in Christ’s redemptive work that must be supplied, which would threaten the doctrine of the sufficiency of Christ’s atonement.  
- The classic and better-attested reading is that Paul speaks of “participatory” or “experiential” sufferings — his own bodily hardships that continue the pattern of Christ’s sufferings in the life and mission of the church.

My position and why (one sentence): I hold the participatory reading — Christ’s atonement is once-for-all and fully sufficient (Hebrews 10:10-14 BSB; Acts 20:28 BSB), while Paul’s language speaks of sharing in Christ’s sufferings as a member of his body, completing the experiential/missional aspect of Christ’s afflictions until the church is brought to maturity and the gospel is proclaimed (Philippians 3:10 BSB; Romans 8:17 BSB; 2 Corinthians 4:10-11 BSB; Colossians 1:25-29 BSB). This preserves the doctrines of penal substitution and the sufficiency of Christ’s work while honoring biblical teaching about union with Christ and the believer’s call to share in his sufferings.

One practical next step: read Colossians 1:24-29 and Philippians 3:10 slowly, ask God to show how you share in Christ’s sufferings (for courage, service, or witness), and consider speaking with a pastor or mature believer about how to bear those burdens faithfully. Would you like a short devotional prompt on Colossians 1:24-29 to help you reflect?
How can I get my prayers answered by God?

Short answer: God answers prayer when we come to him in faith, repentant and submitted to his will, and pray through Jesus; his answer may be yes, no, or wait because he is sovereign and wise. 1 John 5:14-15; Hebrews 11:6

How this plays out (briefly and biblically)
- Trust and faith, not mere wishful thinking. God listens to prayer offered in faith. Hebrews 11:6
- Pray according to God’s will and in Jesus’ name. We have access to the Father through Christ and should ask in line with his will. John 14:13-14; 1 John 5:14-15; Hebrews 4:16
- Confess known sin and be reconciled. Unrepentant sin hinders fellowship and effective prayer. 1 John 1:9; Psalm 66:18
- Forgive others. Harboring unforgiveness is a barrier to answered prayer. Mark 11:25
- Examine motives. Selfish desires, not God‑glorifying ones, can lead to unanswered requests. James 4:3
- Persevere and expect growth. Jesus taught persistent prayer, and the Spirit intercedes for us when we do not know what to pray. Luke 18:1; Romans 8:26
- Remember God’s sovereignty and good purposes, even when the answer is not what you want. God works for our good and his glory. Romans 8:28

One concrete next step you can try tonight
- Set a 15‑minute time: (1) Quietly confess any known sin, (2) thank God for Christ and any small evidences of his care, (3) make one clear request (state it out loud), asking for God’s will to be done, (4) pray in Jesus’ name, and (5) end in quiet submission to his timing. Do this for a week and keep a simple prayer journal of what you asked, how you felt, and any answers or changes in your heart. 1 John 1:9; John 14:13-14; Luke 18:1

Pastoral encouragement
Prayer is primarily fellowship with your Father, not a formula. Keep coming to him with honesty and humility, lean on the Spirit, and stay in the Word and the body of Christ so your prayers are shaped by God’s promises. Hebrews 4:16; Romans 8:26

What is the more excellent way (1 Corinthians 12:31)?

Short answer: Paul’s “more excellent way” is love, the self‑giving, God‑like love (agape) that orders and gives meaning to all spiritual gifts.

Why: In 1 Corinthians Paul teaches that spiritual gifts (charismata) are given for the common good, yet without love they are empty, noisy, or even harmful, so he moves from a catalog of gifts to a chapter defining love as the supreme ethic and goal, 1 Corinthians 13:1-13, with the point that love endures when some gifts cease, and so is the excellence we must pursue, 1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Corinthians 13:8-13. Theologically this ties to sanctification and the fruit of the Spirit—Christlikeness shown in sacrificial service and unity, Galatians 5:22-23; John 13:34-35.

Practical next step: this week pick one concrete way to show patient, humble, practical love to someone in your church or home (for example: listen without defending, serve an unmet need, or speak an encouraging truth), asking the Holy Spirit to make your motive Gospel‑shaped, and meditate on 1 Corinthians 13:4-7.

Scripture references: 1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Corinthians 12:31; 1 Corinthians 13:1-13; Galatians 5:22-23; John 13:34-35.

What is the open heaven concept?

Briefly: the "open heaven" concept is a biblical image describing occasions when the heavens are visibly or spiritually opened and God’s presence, revelation, blessing, or angelic activity break into our world, signaling divine initiative, commissioning, or outpouring of grace (for example Ezekiel 1:1; Matthew 3:16; Acts 7:56; Revelation 4:1; Genesis 7:11; Malachi 3:10, BSB). Theologically this name points to revelation and providential intervention, often linked to angelic ministry, prophetic activity, or seasons of blessing and mission.

How Christians discuss it today, briefly:
- Continuationist/charismatic view in one sentence: Christians who hold continuationism say "open heavens" describes present-day seasons of prophetic vision, miraculous signs, and increased access to God’s presence, as promised in Joel and seen at Pentecost (Joel 2:28–29; Acts 2:17–18, BSB).  
- Cessationist view in one sentence: Christians who hold cessationism warn that extraordinary revelatory gifts that characterized the apostolic age have ceased, so modern claims of continual "open heavens" must be tested and not trusted as new public revelation (1 John 4:1, BSB).  
- My position in one sentence and why: I hold the Reformed Baptist/cessationist caution, because Scripture and the completed apostolic witness give the church sufficient, normative revelation and we must avoid elevating private visions above the Bible while still praying for God’s gracious presence and blessing.

Pastoral application: if you are experiencing or hearing about an "open heavens" event, read and compare the biblical texts like Joel 2:28–29 and Acts 2:17–18, test experiences by Scripture and church wisdom (1 John 4:1), and bring them before trusted elders or mature believers for discernment. Would you like short notes on the key biblical passages or help framing questions to discuss with your pastor?
What is the origin of baptism?

Brief answer: Baptism has its roots in Old Testament ceremonial washings and Second Temple Jewish cleansing practices, appears in the ministry of John the Baptist as a call to repentance, and is instituted as the Christian ordinance by Christ and received by the early church as a sign and seal of union with him. 

Why and how the Bible presents that origin, with key passages:
- Old Testament types of cleansing and purification provided the background (for example, priestly washings in Leviticus 16:4; and the prophetic promise of spiritual cleansing in Ezekiel 36:25).  
- John the Baptist introduced baptism as an urgent call to repentance in preparation for the Messiah (Matthew 3:1-6; John 1:29-34).  
- Jesus himself was baptized and thereby affirmed the practice (Matthew 3:13-17), and he commanded his disciples to make disciples and baptize them (Matthew 28:19-20).  
- The apostles practiced baptism in the new covenant era (Acts 2:38-41; Acts 8:36-39), and the New Testament explains baptism theologically as portraying union with Christ in his death, burial, and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4; Colossians 2:11-12).

A brief linguistic and doctrinal note: the Greek verb baptizo normally means to immerse or dip, and the New Testament practice (for example, going down into the water and coming up again in Acts 8:38-39) fits immersion. Theologically, baptism in historic Protestant (including Reformed Baptist) thought is a covenant sign and seal pointing to regeneration by grace through faith, not a means that automatically saves; salvation is by faith alone in Christ alone (Romans 6:3-4).

Actionable next step: read Matthew 3, Matthew 28, Acts 2, and Romans 6 in the Berean Standard Bible, then talk with a pastor in a Bible-teaching local church about what baptism means, who should be baptized, and how your church practices it. Would you like a short summary of how different Christian traditions interpret baptism (infant vs believer, mode, meaning)?
What is historical criticism?

Historical criticism, often called the historical-critical method, is the scholarly approach that studies the Bible’s human authorship, historical setting, literary forms, and development in order to recover the original meaning intended by the authors. It uses techniques such as source criticism, form criticism, redaction criticism, and attention to ancient backgrounds (for example Second Temple Jewish and Greco-Roman contexts) to explain how biblical books took their present shape.

Why it matters for Christians: historical tools can illuminate genre, idiom, and historical references so that we read the text more accurately, but some forms of the method adopt methodological naturalism and skepticism about miracles or divine inspiration, which can conflict with the Bible’s claim to be God‑breathed. The Bible itself is our final authority: 2 Timothy 3:16-17; Acts 17:11 offers a model of testing teaching against Scripture; Psalm 119:160 affirms the enduring truth of God’s words.

Two historic Christian responses in one sentence each:
- Many conservative scholars use historical-critical tools prudently, subjecting findings to the doctrine of inspiration and the inerrancy/authority of Scripture. 2 Timothy 3:16-17.
- Some liberal or secular scholars apply the method with methodological naturalism and therefore read the text apart from its theological claims, which can lead to skeptical conclusions about miracles and historicity.

My position briefly: historical criticism can be a useful instrument for faithful interpretation when it is subordinated to sola Scriptura and the orthodox confession (for example, the deity and resurrection of Christ); it must not be allowed to replace the Bible’s own claim to be God’s authoritative Word. Use the method to clarify context and meaning, not to decide theological truth apart from the text itself.

A practical next step: read a balanced introduction (for example, a conservative primer on biblical interpretation) and compare its historical observations with the text itself, then discuss questions with a trusted pastor or teacher.
What does the Bible say about paying taxes?

Brief answer: The Bible teaches Christians to submit to governing authorities and to pay lawful taxes as part of our duty to the common good, while always placing obedience to God above human commands. Romans 13 teaches the general rule; Jesus’ “Render to Caesar” teaching shows the proper distinction between obligations to the state and obligations to God.

What Scripture says, with key texts
- Christians are to submit to the state and pay what is due, because governing authorities are instituted by God and serve the common good, including collecting taxes. Romans 13:1-7 BSB.  
- Jesus taught that we should “give to Caesar what is Caesar’s” while giving to God what is God’s, distinguishing civic obligations from our ultimate allegiance to God. Matthew 22:15-22 BSB; Mark 12:13-17 BSB; Luke 20:20-26 BSB.  
- Jesus even provided for paying the temple tax to avoid unnecessary offense, showing pastoral prudence in civic matters. Matthew 17:24-27 BSB.  
- Peter exhorts believers to submit to every human institution for the Lord’s sake. 1 Peter 2:13-17 BSB.  
- If the state commands disobedience to God, Christians must obey God rather than men, as the apostles declared. Acts 5:29 BSB.

Theological significance in one sentence
- Paying taxes is an expression of Christian stewardship and submission to God-ordained authorities, while our highest loyalty remains to God alone, which may require principled resistance if the state commands sinful acts.

Practical applications (concrete)
- File and pay taxes honestly, avoid tax evasion, and view civic obedience as part of Christian witness.  
- Pray for leaders and participate lawfully in the political process to seek justice and the common good.  
- If a law or tax requirement would force you to violate God’s commands, refuse that evil, seek counsel from your pastor and legal advisers, and be prepared to accept lawful consequences, following Acts 5:29 BSB.

Actionable next step
- This week, pray for your civil leaders, review your most recent tax return for accuracy, and if you have a conscience concern about a tax or law, bring it to a trusted church elder or a Christian legal counselor for biblically grounded guidance.

If you want, I can explain how these passages have been applied historically by Christians, or help you work through a specific tax conscience issue you’re facing.
Should Christian women wear pants?

Short answer: Pants themselves are not a gospel issue—Scripture’s clear commands focus on avoiding cross-dressing and on modest, respectable apparel, and Christians should apply Christian liberty and conscience to clothing choices (see Deuteronomy 22:5 BSB; 1 Timothy 2:9 BSB; Romans 14:1 BSB).

Two historic, charitable positions:
- Many churches and believers say women may wear pants, arguing that clothing norms are culturally conditioned and that the primary biblical requirements are modesty and godly character.  
- Other churches maintain women should not wear pants because they view pants as traditionally masculine clothing and appeal to Deuteronomy 22:5 to protect gender distinctions.

My position (one faithful option): I believe women may wear pants, exercising Christian liberty while pursuing sanctification—avoid imitating men in a way that aims to deny or confuse God-given gender distinctions, prioritize modesty and godly witness, and be sensitive to the conscience of your local church and weaker brothers and sisters (Romans 14:1 BSB; 1 Corinthians 10:31 BSB). This balances the biblical commands against cross-dressing and for modesty (Deuteronomy 22:5 BSB; 1 Timothy 2:9 BSB) with the New Testament call to freedom in Christ governed by love and conscience.

Practical next step: Pray for wisdom, read Deuteronomy 22:5 and 1 Timothy 2:9, and talk with your pastor or a trusted mature believer about how your choice of clothing affects ministry, family, and local church conscience—if you tell me your specific context (worship, workplace, family expectations), I can help apply these principles more concretely.
Why wasn't Jesus named Immanuel?

Short answer: Because Immanuel (Hebrew, “God with us”) is a prophetic title describing who Jesus is in his person, while his actual, commanded name was Jesus (Yeshua), which announces his saving work, “Yahweh saves.” Matthew sees Isaiah’s Immanuel prophecy fulfilled in the person of Jesus even though the child’s given name is Jesus.

Explanation and Scripture: Isaiah foretold a sign, “Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign, Behold, the virgin will conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel” (Isaiah 7:14 BSB). Matthew quotes that prophecy of Immanuel to show its fulfillment in Christ, “Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel” (Matthew 1:23 BSB), but the angel told Joseph what his legal name must be, “you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins” (Matthew 1:21 BSB). Luke records the same command to Mary, “You will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to name him Jesus” (Luke 1:31 BSB). Theologically this fits the incarnation and the hypostatic union: Jesus is truly God with us (Immanuel, John 1:14 BSB), and his given name declares his mission as Savior (Jesus, Yeshua, Matthew 1:21 BSB; see Hebrews 1:3 BSB; Colossians 2:9 BSB).

Practical application: meditate on both truths this week—read Isaiah 7 and Matthew 1, thanking God that the child called Jesus is indeed Immanuel, God with us; as a next step, tell God in prayer one way you need his presence or his saving help today. If you’d like, I can guide a short Scripture reading plan or explain more about how Matthew uses Old Testament prophecy.
How is choosing a good name better than choosing riches (Proverbs 22:1)?

Proverbs 22:1 says, "A good name is more desirable than great riches; favor is better than silver and gold" (Proverbs 22:1 BSB). The proverb teaches that a good reputation—rooted in integrity, honor, and faithful character—is more valuable and longer lasting than material wealth.

Why a good name is better than riches:
- A good name buys trust and relationships that wealth cannot secure, and it protects you in times of trouble because people credit your word and character (Proverbs 10:9 BSB; Proverbs 11:3 BSB).  
- Wealth is fragile and can be lost or become a snare; riches may tempt pride, greed, and compromise, while a good name reflects sanctification and obedience to God (Luke 12:15 BSB; 1 Timothy 6:10 BSB).  
- A good name honors God and strengthens your witness for Christ, whereas riches alone do not change the heart or reconcile you to God (Psalm 112:5 BSB; Matthew 5:16 BSB).

Practical application (one next step):

What is the lust of the eyes?

Briefly: the "lust of the eyes" is the sinful craving or covetous desire that arises when something attractive is seen and then wanted above God and His will, a temptation to possess or enjoy what pleases the sight rather than honoring God. 1 John 2:16 names it alongside the lust of the flesh and the pride of life as a root of sin, and Genesis 3:6 gives the classic example where Eve saw the fruit was pleasing to the eye and desired it.

Theology and application in one line, using a technical term: this is a form of concupiscence or covetousness that the Christian must fight in the work of sanctification, because unchecked visual desire leads to sinful actions (James 1:14-15). Practical steps: confess the desire, remove or limit visual occasions of temptation (filters, social-media fasts, avoiding certain stores or sites), replace what you look at with Scripture and gratitude, and enlist an accountability partner or pastor; remember Jesus’ teaching to store up heavenly treasures not earthly ones (Matthew 6:19-21).

Scripture references: 1 John 2:16; Genesis 3:6; James 1:14-15; Matthew 6:19-21.

What does the Bible say about Voodoo? How should a Christian view Voodoo?

Short answer: The Bible does not name “Voodoo,” but it clearly forbids the kinds of practices Voodoo uses—sorcery, divination, medium‑calling, ancestor spirit veneration, and idolatry—and instructs God’s people to turn from them. Deuteronomy 18:9-12; Leviticus 19:31; Leviticus 20:6,27; Isaiah 8:19.

What Scripture teaches (briefly and by category)
- Forbidden practices: God forbids consulting mediums, practicing divination, witchcraft, or necromancy because they seek knowledge and power apart from the Lord. Deuteronomy 18:9-12; Leviticus 19:31; Leviticus 20:27.
- Biblical examples and warnings: Saul’s seeking the medium at Endor is judged (1 Samuel 28), and the New Testament treats “sorcery” as a work of the flesh to be abandoned (Galatians 5:19-21); converts in Acts publicly renounced and destroyed occult books (Acts 19:19).
- Spiritual reality and danger: Scripture links these practices to idolatry and to spiritual powers opposed to God, so they expose people to deception and harm (Ephesians 6:10-12; Colossians 2:15).
- Final judgment: Scripture includes sorcerers among those judged (Revelation 21:8; Revelation 22:15).

Theological summary in one sentence
- Voodoo’s practices fall under biblical categories of spiritism, sorcery, and idolatry, which Scripture condemns because they replace trust in the one true God with other spirits and powers (Deuteronomy 18:10-12; Galatians 5:19-21).

How a Christian should view Voodoo—pastoral and practical guidance
- Do not participate. Scripture gives a clear prohibition and calls believers to avoid occult practices (Leviticus 19:31; Deuteronomy 18:9-12).
- Recognize the spiritual danger and Christ’s victory. Jesus has defeated the powers that oppress sinners, so Christians should rely on Christ’s lordship, not on occult practices (Colossians 2:15; Romans 6:10-11).
- Love the people who follow Voodoo. Treat them with compassion, not contempt; share the gospel as the true remedy for sin and spiritual bondage (Ephesians 4:15; Romans 1:16).
- If you or someone you know is involved: repent, confess, and turn to Christ; seek a pastor or mature Christian for discipleship and counseling rather than pursuing occult or sensational “deliverance” techniques. Galatians 5:19-21; James 5:16.

A single, clear gospel invitation (brief)
- The Bible says all have sinned and need a Savior; Christ died and rose to forgive sins—turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; Romans 10:9-10). 

One practical next step
- If you are personally involved in Voodoo or concerned about someone who is, ask your local pastor or a trusted elder for a confidential conversation this week, and begin reading Scripture daily (start with the Gospel of John) while praying for wisdom and courage (John 20:31; Philippians 4:6-7).

What is the meaning of Lent?

Short answer: Lent is the roughly 40-day season observed by many Christians before Easter as a time of repentance, fasting, and focused devotion leading to remembrance of Christ’s suffering, death, and resurrection.

What it is and why it matters, briefly
- It is a church season, not a biblical command, and therefore a non‑essential practice where faithful Christians may differ. The season is intended to cultivate repentance, spiritual discipline, and attention to the cross and resurrection.
- Biblical patterns that undergird the practice include Jesus’ 40 days of fasting (Matthew 4:1-2), the call to repent (Joel 2:12-13), teaching about fasting (Matthew 6:16-18), and the regular remembrance of Christ’s death and resurrection (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

Two common Christian approaches
- Liturgical view (Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, many Anglican and Lutheran churches): Lent is a formal, 40-day penitential season with prescribed fasts, liturgies, and disciplines intended to prepare hearts for Holy Week and Easter.  
- Protestant/Reformed responses: some Protestants observe Lent as a voluntary season of disciplined devotion and repentance, while others reject it as an extra‑biblical tradition and prefer keeping every day centered on Christ without a formal liturgical season.

My position (brief)
- As a Reformed Baptist position, I view Lent as a useful, voluntary spiritual discipline when it points believers to Christ, calls people to repentance, and fosters reliance on the Spirit, but I reject any suggestion that observing Lent is required for salvation or gives merit before God, because salvation is by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

One concrete way to observe Lent if you want to try it
- Choose one focused practice for 40 days, for example: read one chapter from a Gospel each day concentrating on the Passion narratives, pray daily for forgiveness and renewal, fast one meal per week, and meet weekly with a friend or elder for accountability. As you do this, draw near to God’s throne of grace for help and mercy (Hebrews 4:14-16).

Would you like a simple 40-day reading plan through the Passion narratives or a short daily liturgy you can use personally or with a small group?
What doth every sin deserve?

Every sin doth deserve God’s righteous wrath and eternal condemnation for sin, because all have sinned and sin’s just wage is death. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; Romans 1:18.

The Bible also teaches that this penalty is real and final for the unrepentant, awaiting the judgment of God. John 3:36; Revelation 20:11-15.

But the gospel is the good news that Christ bore the penalty our sins deserved, satisfying divine justice by his substitutionary death and rising again, so that sinners are justified by faith. 2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24; Romans 5:8-9. This is the doctrine of penal substitution and justification by faith.

A practical next step: confess your sin to God, repent, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life, and connect with a Bible-teaching local church for discipleship. If you want, I can help you understand how to do that or pray through what repentance and trusting Christ practically look like. Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.
What does the Bible say about diligence?

Brief answer: The Bible praises diligence as wise, faithful, and obedient work that flows from a heart renewed by grace; it calls believers to steady effort in work, holiness, and service as part of sanctification and faithful stewardship. Diligence is not a way to earn salvation, but a Christian duty and fruit of God’s work in us.

What Scripture teaches (key themes with short explanation and prooftexts):
- Diligence is wise, leads to blessing, while laziness brings loss, in daily life and provision, as Proverbs repeatedly teaches. Proverbs 10:4; Proverbs 12:24; Proverbs 13:4; Proverbs 21:5.
- Work done for the Lord is meaningful — Christians are to labor as if serving Christ, not merely people, showing diligence in ordinary tasks. Colossians 3:23.
- Diligence is part of faith’s outworking in the life of the believer, pursued alongside spiritual growth. 2 Peter 1:5-10.
- Persevering zeal and earnest effort are required in the Christian life and hope; Christians are urged to be zealous and not lazy. Romans 12:11; Hebrews 6:11-12.
- Christ’s parable of the talents teaches faithful stewardship of gifts and opportunities, rewarding diligent faithfulness. Matthew 25:14-30.
- Our striving is sanctified by God’s enabling power: we “work out” salvation in obedience because God works in us to will and to act. Philippians 2:12-13.
- This effort is not the basis of justification; salvation is by grace through faith, not by works, but true faith bears fruit. Ephesians 2:8-9; James 2:17.

Theological summary in one sentence:
Diligence is a sanctification and stewardship virtue—an obedient, persevering practice empowered by the Spirit and flowing from justifying faith, not a means of earning God’s acceptance (sanctification, stewardship, perseverance, justification).

One practical step you can take this week:
Choose one regular task (work, study, family duty, or spiritual discipline), set a small measurable goal for it each day, pray briefly before beginning asking God for strength, and review progress at the week’s end while thanking God for any growth; this trains faithful habits and dependence on the Lord. (See Colossians 3:23; Philippians 2:12-13.)

If you’d like, tell me where you want more diligence (work, family, Bible reading, holiness), and I’ll suggest a 7‑day practical plan and a verse to memorize to encourage you.
What is agnosticism?

Agnosticism is the position that the existence or nature of God (or ultimate reality) is unknown or unknowable. It can mean simply that one withholds belief because of insufficient evidence (epistemic agnosticism), or it can claim that human beings are in principle unable to know God (strong or philosophical agnosticism); some people use the term to describe a practical stance of living as if God does not exist.

How it differs from other positions: theist affirms God exists, atheist denies God exists, and agnostic suspends judgment or doubts that knowledge is possible. Related technical terms are epistemology (the study of knowledge) and fideism (trust without evidence), which help explain why people arrive at different conclusions.

Biblical significance and response: Christians affirm that God has made himself known, both through general revelation and through Christ, so the Bible treats knowledge of God as accessible, though it also calls for faith. See Romans 1:20; John 1:1-4; Acts 17:27; Hebrews 11:1.

A practical next step: if you are exploring this question, read Romans 1:20 and John 1:1-4, ask God for insight, and talk with a trusted Christian (pastor or mature believer) about the reasons people say agnosticism is attractive and how the Gospel answers the deepest questions. Would you like me to outline common arguments for agnosticism and the Christian responses to them?
What does it mean to “be angry and do not sin” (Psalm 4:4)?

Short answer: The phrase means that feeling anger can be morally neutral or even appropriate when confronted with sin, injustice, or grief, but that anger must be controlled by holiness—do not let it become sinful (bitterness, revenge, uncontrolled words, or loss of trust in God). Psalm 4:4 and Ephesians 4:26 teach the same restraint: feel, but do not sin in the feeling or in how you act on it. Psalm 4:4; Ephesians 4:26.

Why this matters biblically: Scripture affirms that God himself is angry at sin (God’s holy wrath), so our conscience can rightly be provoked when God’s good order is violated; yet the Bible repeatedly warns that human anger, left unchecked, breeds unrighteousness (for example, James warns that human anger does not produce God’s righteousness). James 1:19-20; Psalm 37:8; Romans 12:17-19.

What “do not sin” looks like in practice
- Do not repay evil with vengeance, intentionally harm, or nurse secret grudges (Romans 12:17-19).  
- Do not let anger drive you to slander, rash words, or a refusal to forgive (Ephesians 4:31-32).  
- Do not allow anger to harden into despair or a loss of trust in God; instead let it lead you to grief over sin and to dependence on Christ for change (sanctification).

One concrete next step: the next time you feel anger, pause and pray (even a short prayer: “Lord, is this righteous grief or selfish rage? Help me”), speak truth to yourself from Scripture, and if the anger involves another person, aim to address it quickly, humbly, and biblically (Ephesians 4:26 says not to let the sun go down on your anger). Ephesians 4:26; James 1:19-20.

Pastoral encouragement: God cares about your heart and will help you grow in holy anger and in mercy—ask him to reveal any sinful motives, confess them, and seek repentance and reconciliation through the power of Christ (Hebrews 4:15-16; Philippians 2:12-13). Hebrews 4:15-16; Philippians 2:12-13.

How will the covenant with death be annulled (Isaiah 28:18)?

Brief answer: The “covenant with death” in Isaiah 28:18 is annulled when God breaks the false assurance of those trusting in death and Sheol and executes his decisive judgment and deliverance, ultimately accomplished in Christ’s atoning death, victorious resurrection, and final judgment which breaks death’s power. Isaiah’s prophecy points forward to God’s judicial overthrow of any reliance on death and to the gospel victory that removes death’s sting (atonement, resurrection, final judgment).

Why this follows from the text and the Bible, briefly:
- Isaiah’s immediate context condemns leaders who make a proud “covenant with death” and promises God will make that counsel of death void when his judgment comes, not by human trickery but by the Lord’s righteous intervention, Isaiah 28:15–18. Isaiah 28:18.  
- The Old Testament elsewhere pictures Yahweh destroying death’s victory, Isaiah 25:8, and the New Testament shows that defeat carried out in Christ: his substitutionary atoning death, burial, and bodily resurrection remove death’s claim and secure final victory for his people (1 Corinthians 15:54–57; Romans 6:9). Isaiah 25:8; 1 Corinthians 15:54–57; Romans 6:9.  
- The consummation is described as death and Hades being cast into the lake of fire at the final judgment, when death’s dominion is ended forever, Revelation 20:14. Revelation 20:14.

Practical application: If you find yourself leaning on a false security (wealth, politics, human schemes), repent and place your trust in Jesus Christ whose cross and resurrection annul death’s claim and give real life; as a next step, read Isaiah 28:12–22 and 1 Corinthians 15:1–8, pray for faith, and speak with a pastor or a mature believer about what trusting Christ looks like in your life.

What is the Hindu caste system?

Short answer: The Hindu caste system is a historical social ordering in South Asia that sorts people into heredity-based groups (broad varṇa categories and many local jāti groups), assigning social status, customary occupations, marriage rules, and ideas of ritual purity and pollution. Though its religious texts and historical practices vary, it has shaped everyday life, politics, and inequality in India and the diaspora and continues to have social effects despite legal prohibitions.

Key points
- Varṇa (class) — a fourfold ideal found in late Vedic texts: Brahmin (priests, teachers), Kshatriya (warriors, rulers), Vaishya (merchants, farmers), and Shudra (servants, laborers). The Purusha Sukta hymn in the Rigveda is often cited as a textual source for this schema.
- Jāti (birth‑group) — the much more numerous, locally organized endogamous groups that actually govern marriage, occupation, and social networks in most communities; jāti is the lived social reality far more than the abstract varṇa.
- Untouchability and Dalits — certain groups were historically excluded and stigmatized as “untouchable”; the term Dalit is used by many members of those communities and movements for social justice and emancipation.
- Origins and development — scholars note varṇa ideals in Vedic literature but debate how and when social roles hardened into rigid, hereditary caste; later texts like Manusmriti helped codify rules about status and conduct, and over centuries the system varied widely by region and era.
- Colonial and modern change — British colonial administration (censuses, law) and modern political developments helped freeze some categories and make caste politically salient; independent India’s constitution outlaws untouchability (Article 17) and creates affirmative‑action (reservation) categories for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and Other Backward Classes, but social discrimination and inequality persist.
- Contemporary reality — caste still affects marriage, politics, economic opportunity, and social life in many places; at the same time, social reform movements (Bhakti saints, Sikhism and Buddhism, reformers like B R Ambedkar and Mahatma Gandhi), urbanization, education, and law have reduced its power in many contexts.
- Global dimension — caste identities and tensions have traveled with the South Asian diaspora and sometimes surface in workplaces, religious communities, and social organizations abroad.

If you’d like to go deeper, I can suggest readable introductions and primary texts (for example, B R Ambedkar’s Annihilation of Caste and Nicholas Dirks’s Castes of Mind) or explain how caste interacts with politics, law, or everyday life in a particular region. Which angle interests you most?
What does the Bible say about rest and relaxation?

Briefly: the Bible teaches that rest is both a created good and a commanded, holy rhythm for God’s people, and that true soul-rest is ultimately found in Christ. This means rest is theological (Sabbath and entering God’s rest), practical (physical recuperation and recreation), and spiritual (trust, dependence, and sanctification).

Key biblical truths (with Scripture):
- Creation instituted rest, blessing a day of ceasing from creative work, giving a pattern for human rhythm, Genesis 2:2–3 BSB.  
- God commanded a Sabbath-keeping rhythm for Israel as a covenantal sign of trust and worship, Exodus 20:8–11 BSB.  
- Jesus invites the weary to find rest for their souls in him, promising his yoke is easy and his burden light, Matthew 11:28–30 BSB.  
- The New Testament teaches there remains a Sabbath-rest for God’s people and urges us to strive to enter that rest, connecting Sabbath with final salvation and ongoing trust, Hebrews 4:9–11 BSB.  
- Scripture affirms rest and renewal in pastoral, physical, and relational ways (for example, Jesus sent his disciples away to rest briefly), Mark 6:31 BSB; and the Psalms picture God as one who leads us beside quiet waters and restores the soul, Psalm 23:1–3 BSB; Psalm 62:1–2 BSB.  
- Rest is not an excuse for idleness; Christians are called to faithful work done to the Lord, Colossians 3:23–24 BSB, and warned against willful laziness, 2 Thessalonians 3:10 BSB.  
- We care for our bodies as temples and steward physical health and renewal as part of Christian stewardship, 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 BSB.

Practical application (one next step):
- Start with a tangible Sabbath rhythm this week: pick a 3–6 hour block (or a full day when possible), turn off work devices, spend time in Scripture and prayer, rest physically (sleep, walk, or sit quietly), enjoy restorative fellowship or recreation, and reflect on trusting God rather than productivity. If you need help shaping this, talk with a pastor or an elder in your church for accountability and biblical guidance.

A pastoral word: God’s gift of rest both refreshes the body and trains our hearts to trust him—go gently into that rest and ask him to teach you its rhythms as part of your sanctification, Hebrews 4:9–11 BSB; Matthew 11:28–30 BSB. What is deism? What do deists believe?

Short answer: Deism is the belief that God exists as the Creator but does not intervene in the world after creation. Deists hold that reason and the observation of nature, rather than revealed Scripture or miracles, are the primary ways to know God.

Core elements many deists affirm
- God as a transcendent Creator who set the universe in motion but does not act supernaturally in history (the “watchmaker” idea).
- Natural religion and natural theology, meaning moral truths and knowledge of God are available through reason and the created order rather than special revelation.
- Rejection of supernatural revelation, prophecy, and miracles as reliable sources of divine truth.
- Skepticism or rejection of historic Christian claims about the incarnation, atoning sacrifice, and supernatural resurrection of Jesus; some deists treat Jesus as a moral teacher, others sideline him entirely.
- Moral life grounded in human reason and conscience rather than sacramental or revealed obligations.
- Varied beliefs about the afterlife, ranging from belief in simple reward and punishment to agnosticism about immortality.

Brief historical note
- Deism rose in the 17th and 18th centuries among Enlightenment thinkers who prized reason and wanted religion purged of what they saw as superstition.

How Christians differ (briefly and clearly)
- Christians affirm that God does not merely create and step back, but continually sustains and acts in history, and that God has spoken in special revelation supremely in the person and work of Jesus Christ. For example, Scripture teaches God has spoken through the Son, who sustains all things, Hebrews 1:1-3; and the Gospel announces Christ’s death, burial, and resurrection for our salvation, 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 3:23-26; John 1:14. Because deism denies the necessity and reality of special revelation and the saving work of Christ, it is not an orthodox Christian position.

If you want to explore further
- Read Psalm 19:1-4 to see the Bible’s affirmation that creation reveals God, and then read John to see the Christian claim that God also revealed himself in Christ; Psalm 19:1-4; John 1:14.
- If you are weighing worldviews, a practical step is to read the Gospel of John and talk with a pastor or a mature Christian you trust about the differences you notice.

If you’d like, I can summarize common objections deists raise and how Christians respond, or give a short, gentle presentation of the Gospel. Would you prefer that?
Is the distinction between clergy and laity biblical?

Short answer: The New Testament clearly sets apart and appoints certain believers to oversight and service roles (elders/overseers and deacons), but it does not teach a permanent, ontological two-tier spiritual caste called “clergy” wholly separate from the rest of the people of God (see Ephesians 4:11-12; 1 Peter 2:9).

Historic opposing view in one sentence: Many Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and some Anglican traditions affirm an ordained, sacramental priesthood and episcopal succession that locates a distinct, ordered clergy with sacramental authority and a special ontological status apart from the laity.

My position and why in one sentence: I hold the Reformed Baptist/evangelical balance that Scripture authorizes distinct, biblically ordained offices—elders (pastors/overseers) and deacons—for oversight and shepherding while upholding the priesthood of all believers, so ministry is shared by the whole church even as elders bear particular pastoral and teaching responsibility (see Acts 6:1-6; 1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9; 1 Peter 2:9).

A few clarifying points and practical application:
- Theological term: the Bible teaches both the office of elder/overseer (episkopos) and the priesthood of all believers (1 Peter 2:9), so authority is functional and pastoral rather than creating a separate spiritual caste.  
- Pastoral teaching and submission are commanded (Hebrews 13:7; Hebrews 13:17), but that submission is to shepherding overseers, not to infallible human pronouncements.  
- Concrete application: If you want clarity about your local church, ask the elders how they understand eldership, request their church polity statement, and read Acts 6 and 1 Timothy 3 with them to see how office and common priesthood work together.

If you like, tell me whether you attend a church that uses the terms “clergy” and “laity,” and I can suggest specific questions to bring to your elders or a short Bible-reading plan to study these passages together.
What does it mean to have a wilderness experience?

Briefly: a "wilderness experience" is a season in which God allows or leads a person into hardship, isolation, or testing in order to humbly strip away self-reliance, reveal the heart, and form trust and obedience (sanctification). 

Biblical pattern and meaning:
- Israel wandered in the wilderness as God tested and humbled them, teaching dependence on his word and provision (Deuteronomy 8:2-3 BSB).  
- Jesus was led into the wilderness to be tempted and proved, showing that testing can prepare one for ministry and deepen reliance on the Father (Matthew 4:1-11 BSB).  
- Elijah’s wilderness episode shows that in a place of apparent defeat God meets, feeds, and restores his servant (1 Kings 19:4-8 BSB).  
- The wilderness can be both consequence and mercy: it may come after sin or failure, yet God uses it as discipline and refining for growth (Hebrews 12:5-11 BSB). For comfort, even in the darkest valleys God is present with us (Psalm 23:4 BSB).

What this looks like in life:
- Inner experiences: loneliness, confusion, temptation, spiritual dryness, or loss of former certainties.  
- Spiritual purposes: testing of faith, removal of idols, deeper dependence on Christ, preparation for new calling, or pastoral restoration. Theologically it’s part of progressive sanctification and God’s providential discipline aimed at our good (Romans 8:28 BSB).

If you’re in a wilderness now — a practical next step:
- Honestly bring the situation to God and to a trusted, mature believer or pastor; ask them to pray with you, read Deuteronomy 8:2-3 and Matthew 4:1-11 together, and help you identify one small, obedient step to take this week (for example, confess a specific sin, reestablish a Bible-and-prayer rhythm, or serve someone in need).

A final word of hope: the wilderness is seldom the end of the story—God often leads his people through it to greater dependence on Christ and renewed fruitfulness (Romans 8:28; Psalm 23:4 BSB). If you want, tell me briefly what your wilderness feels like and I’ll pray for you and suggest a specific next step.
What does Malachi 2:16 mean when God says, ‘I hate divorce’?

Brief answer: God’s statement “I hate divorce” (Malachi 2:16) means he strongly opposes the breaking of the marriage covenant because it is treachery against a spouse and harms the vulnerable; it is a denunciation of the sin and violence that often attend divorce, not a capricious personal dislike. Malachi condemns men who break faith with their wives and treat them violently, and God declares his fierce rejection of that practice. Malachi 2:13-16.

Why this language? In the Bible “hate” can be emphatic language showing absolute rejection of an action that violates God’s covenant purposes, especially when that action injures the weak. Marriage is a covenant designed to be lifelong (Genesis 2:24), and the prophets and the New Testament reassert God’s intent for faithfulness (Matthew 19:3-9; Ephesians 5:25-33). Malachi’s point is moral and covenantal: divorce, when used as treachery or cruelty, is opposed by God. Malachi 2:13-16; Genesis 2:24; Matthew 19:3-9; Ephesians 5:25-33.

Are there biblical exceptions? Scripture does recognize limited grounds and pastoral responses in a fallen world — Jesus allows divorce in the case of sexual immorality (Matthew 19:9), and Paul gives guidance for abandonment by an unbelieving spouse (1 Corinthians 7:10-16) — but these do not nullify God’s ideal or his hatred of the injustice and violence that often attend divorce. Matthew 19:9; 1 Corinthians 7:10-16.

Pastoral help: If you are facing divorce or its aftermath, seek counsel from your pastor or a wise, biblically faithful Christian counselor, pursue reconciliation where safe and possible, and lean on the gospel for forgiveness and healing (Ephesians 1:7; 2 Corinthians 5:17). Ephesians 5:25-33; Ephesians 1:7; 2 Corinthians 5:17.

How transparent should church finances be?

Short answer: Church finances ought to be substantially transparent to the congregation, subject to elder oversight and independent review, while protecting donor privacy and obeying the law. This reflects biblical stewardship and the need to avoid reproach in handling gifts (Acts 6:1-6; 1 Timothy 3:8-13; Titus 1:7).

One opposing historic view: some churches have practiced limited disclosure, trusting only elders and treasurers with full details to protect confidentiality and preserve unity.

My position and why: I recommend clear, regular transparency—publish an annual budget and an audited or independently reviewed financial statement, provide regular verbal/electronic updates at business meetings, maintain a finance committee under eldership oversight, and keep internal controls (segregation of duties, independent audit)—while excluding donor-identifying details unless donors consent. Transparency honors biblical stewardship, protects the flock from abuse, and helps the church avoid suspicion (2 Corinthians 8:20-21; Proverbs 27:23; Luke 16:10-12; 1 Peter 5:2-3).

Concrete application: practical items to implement—an annual summary of income and expenses, a posted annual budget, documented financial policies, an independent review or audit, and clear lines of authority between elders and staff.

One actionable next step: if you serve in leadership, commission an independent financial review this year and publish a one-page income/expense summary to the congregation; if you are a member concerned about transparency, ask your elders for the most recent budget and audit and request a short Q&A at the next business meeting.

Is intinction the correct way to do communion?

Short answer: Intinction (dipping the bread into the cup) is practiced legitimately in some churches, but Scripture does not prescribe one universal mode—Jesus instituted the meal with bread and a cup (Matthew 26:26-28; Mark 14:22-24; Luke 22:19-20; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

Historic opposing view, briefly: Many Reformed and Baptist traditions prefer using the bread and cup separately, arguing that separate elements more plainly preserve the symbolism of Christ’s body given and blood shed and avoid confusion about how Christ is present in the ordinance.

My position and why: As a Reformed Baptist I favor using the elements separately because the visible distinction of bread and cup fits the plain reading of the institution and guards the ordinance’s meaning (ordination, proclamation of Christ’s atoning work), though I would not make intinction a test of fellowship if a local church’s elders permit it for pastoral reasons. Practically, whether by intinction or separate elements, participants must come reverently and self-examiningly (1 Corinthians 11:27-32), remembering the Lord’s atoning death and bodily resurrection proclaimed in the supper.

Actionable next step: Ask your pastor or elders to explain your congregation’s practice and, if you disagree, discuss it with humility and a willingness to submit to the local church’s ordered worship.
What is the church suffering?

Do you mean the global church today, a local congregation, or your own personal church experience? Briefly: the church suffers persecution, trials, internal sin and false teaching, poverty and marginalization, bodily illness and mourning, and spiritual warfare—all of which Scripture addresses and uses for God’s purposes.

What that looks like, with brief biblical anchors:
- Persecution for the name of Christ, hostility from the world and sometimes imprisonment or loss, as Jesus warned and Scripture records: John 15:18-20; Matthew 5:10-12; Acts 5:41.  
- Trials and testing that refine faith (discipline or proving), including hardship and loss: James 1:2-4; Hebrews 12:3-11.  
- Internal sin, division, and false teaching that wound the body and call for correction: Acts 20:29-30; 2 Peter 2:1.  
- Poverty, marginalization, and social weakness among believers, which the church is called to bear together: James 2:1-5; Romans 15:25-26.  
- Sickness, weakness, and bodily affliction—even “thorns” that Paul experienced—leading Christians to depend on Christ: 2 Corinthians 12:7-10; 2 Corinthians 4:8-12.  
- Spiritual warfare against principalities and powers that attacks believers’ minds and churches: Ephesians 6:12.  
- Loss, grief, and the death of fellow believers, which the church both laments and hopes in Christ’s return: 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18.

Why this matters theologically: Scripture teaches that such suffering is not meaningless but is used by God for sanctification, witness, and sharing in Christ’s sufferings, while pointing forward to glory (sanctification, union with Christ, and the resurrection hope): Romans 8:17-18; 2 Corinthians 4:16-18; Philippians 3:10-11.

A practical, pastoral next step: if you or your church are suffering, bring the situation to your elders or a trusted mature Christian for prayer and wise counsel, name the need in Scripture-centered prayer, and read 1 Peter and Romans for pastoral comfort and direction (for example 1 Peter 4:12-19; Romans 8:28). What does it mean that a person will not inherit the kingdom of God (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)?

Short answer: Paul is warning that people who persist in unrepentant sinful living, the behaviors he lists, will not share in the final, promised rule and blessing of God’s kingdom; immediately he points to the gospel’s power to change lives by saying, “and such were some of you” (1 Corinthians 6:9–11 BSB).

What he means, briefly explained
- “Will not inherit the kingdom of God” refers to final participation in God’s reign and the eternal blessings of salvation, not merely a present social status. Paul’s list of sins functions as a warning that habitual, unrepentant practice of those sins shows a person remains outside God’s saving rule (1 Corinthians 6:9–10 BSB).
- At the same time Paul declares that the gospel changes people: those who turn to Christ are washed, sanctified, and justified — a forensic acquittal and positional newness that begins progressive sanctification in life (1 Corinthians 6:11 BSB; Titus 3:5–7 BSB; Romans 6:1–4 BSB).

Doctrinal balance (truth and love)
- Justification by faith alone means we are declared righteous before God by Christ, not by our works; yet true justification is accompanied by sanctification, a real change in obedience and character (Romans 5:1 BSB; Romans 6:1–4 BSB).
- Paul’s warning is pastoral and prophetic: it calls for repentance. Persistent, unrepentant sin is incompatible with entry into the kingdom; genuine conversion will show itself in a turning from sin and growing faith (1 John 3:6–10 BSB; Philippians 1:6 BSB).

Practical next steps
- If you are worried about where you stand: (1) honestly confess your sin to God and ask for forgiveness, (2) speak with a trustworthy elder or mature believer for counsel and accountability, and (3) begin reading the Gospel of John to see Christ and trust him more each day (1 John 1:9 BSB; James 5:16 BSB; John 20:31 BSB).
- If you have never trusted Christ, turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Is it wise for Christian teens to be dating?

Short answer: It can be, but only when teen relationships are intentional, accountable, and aimed at holiness and preparation for marriage; casual, secretive, or sexually permissive “dating” is unwise.

Why this matters biblically: Scripture calls Christians to sexual purity and to guard the heart, and warns against being unequally yoked with unbelievers, so romantic relationships should serve your sanctification, not endanger it (1 Thessalonians 4:3-5; Proverbs 4:23; 2 Corinthians 6:14).

Two common Christian approaches, fairly stated:
- Some Christians advise delaying romantic dating (favoring family-supervised courtship or waiting until late adolescence/adulthood) to protect purity and allow spiritual growth.
- Other Christians permit supervised, intentional teen dating as a discipleship opportunity to learn covenantal love, emotional maturity, and marriage-readiness under parental and church oversight.

My position (brief): I lean toward structured, accountable relationships rather than casual dating, because teens are still forming morally and emotionally, and the church and parents are biblically given roles to guide and protect young people (Hebrews 13:4; Ephesians 5:21-33). Intentional boundaries and accountability help a relationship produce holiness, not harm, and promote growth in love and self-control (Colossians 3:12-14).

Practical guidelines and one immediate next step:
- Talk with your parents and a pastor or elder before beginning any romantic relationship, agree on clear boundaries (physical, emotional, social), and name accountability partners.  
- Prefer group or public activities, avoid unsupervised alone time, and set clear expectations about communication and social media.  
- Keep spiritual growth central: pray together, read Scripture, attend church and small group, and watch for whether the relationship draws you nearer to Christ.  
- Immediate next step: this week, ask your parent(s) and a trusted church leader for a 20–30 minute conversation to discuss expectations and write down three boundaries you both agree on.

A brief encouragement: God uses relationships to refine us, and He promises to complete the good work He began in us (Philippians 1:6). If you want, I can help you draft the conversation points to take to your parents and pastor.
Why does God allow us to go through trials and tribulations?

Short answer: God allows trials to refine and shape us into Christlikeness, to test and strengthen our faith, to discipline and correct us when needed, and to use our suffering to comfort and serve others while accomplishing his good purposes. James 1:2-4 BSB; Romans 5:3-5 BSB; Hebrews 12:5-11 BSB.

Why this is true, briefly and biblically
- Trials test and produce perseverance and maturity in faith, so we lack nothing spiritually, not to harm but to grow us in sanctification, James 1:2-4 BSB; Romans 5:3-5 BSB.
- God’s fatherly discipline corrects believers for our good, shaping holiness, Hebrews 12:5-11 BSB.
- Trials refine genuine faith like fire refines gold, proving its reality and worth, 1 Peter 1:6-7 BSB.
- Suffering connects us to Christ and prepares us to comfort others from our own experience, 1 Peter 4:12-13 BSB; 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 BSB.
- Even when sin and a fallen world bring pain, God is sovereign and works all things for the good of those who love him, Romans 8:28 BSB; Psalm 34:19 BSB.
- Jesus warned we would have trouble, but he promised his presence and peace in it, John 16:33 BSB.

A few pastoral, practical steps
1) Bring the trial to God in specific prayer and Scripture, asking for wisdom, trust, and clarity; for example, meditate on and memorize James 1:2-4 or Romans 8:28 BSB.  
2) Don’t isolate: tell a trusted elder, friend, or small group and ask them to pray and bear the burden with you, James 5:16 BSB; Hebrews 10:24-25 BSB.  
3) Look for specific ways God might be reshaping you — repent where needed, practice obedience in the small things, and look for opportunities to serve others with what you’re learning.

A short gospel reminder and invitation
We are sinners in need of a Savior, and Jesus Christ lived, died, and rose again to deal with sin and bring us into new life, 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 BSB; 1 Peter 2:24 BSB. Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What does Deuteronomy 10:12 tell us God requires of us?

Deuteronomy 10:12 tells Israel that what the LORD requires is reverent fear of God, walking in his ways, loving him, serving him with all your heart and all your soul, and keeping his commandments (Deuteronomy 10:12–13 BSB).

Biblically this is covenant faithfulness: a call to wholehearted obedience and love (sanctification and obedience rooted in relationship), not mere ritual. The “fear of the LORD” means humble, reverent trust that leads to obedience; Jesus echoes the same demand to love God with all your heart, soul, and mind (Matthew 22:37–40 BSB). Our ability to respond is by God’s grace and the Spirit at work in us (Philippians 2:13 BSB).

Practical step: this week pick one concrete way to “walk in his ways” (for example, a daily act of mercy, truthfulness at work, or time set aside for Scripture and prayer), ask the Spirit to enable you each morning, and review it at night.
