Is it okay to get tattoos if they are of a Christian nature?

Short answer: This is a non-essential matter Christians may disagree about—some see biblical prohibition in Leviticus as culturally specific and permit tasteful Christian tattoos, while others avoid tattoos out of conviction about bodily stewardship or witness. Whatever you decide, examine your motive, conscience, and the effect on others, and seek your church's counsel.

Why Christians differ, briefly:
- View 1 (permissive): Tattoos are allowed under Christian liberty when done with a repentant, Christ-exalting motive, because the New Testament does not give a general ban on markings and freedom in Christ governs disputable matters (Romans 14:1-4; Romans 14:13-23).  
- View 2 (restrictive): Tattoos should be avoided because Leviticus 19:28 appears to forbid marking the body, and because believers are to steward their bodies as the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19-20) and avoid things that may harm witness or cause scandal (1 Corinthians 8:9).

My pastoral position, briefly: I lean toward caution—tattoos are not inherently sinful, but they require careful pastoral testing of motive, permanence, and witness, and one should abstain if conscience, local church conviction, or potential harm to others advises against it (Romans 14:23; 1 Corinthians 10:31).

Biblical and practical reasons to weigh carefully:
- The specific Old Testament prohibition, Leviticus 19:28, was given in a cultural context of pagan mourning and idolatrous practices, so its application requires careful interpretation (Leviticus 19:28).  
- New Testament principles emphasize that our bodies belong to Christ (1 Corinthians 6:19-20), that Christian liberty must be governed by love for weaker consciences (Romans 14:1-4; Romans 14:13-23), and that we should do all to the glory of God (1 Corinthians 10:31). Also avoid causing others to stumble by exercising freedom without love (1 Corinthians 8:9).

Concrete, practical next steps:
1) Pray and ask God to test your motives (Is this to glorify Christ, honor memory, or please myself?), and fast if you sense heavy temptation.  
2) Talk with your pastor or elder and a mature believer in your local church about how a tattoo might affect your testimony and fellowship.  
3) If you proceed, choose a design that clearly honors Christ, is measured in size and placement, and consider long-term implications for family, work, and witness.

Relevant Scripture (BSB): Leviticus 19:28; 1 Corinthians 6:19-20; Romans 14:1-4; Romans 14:13-23; 1 Corinthians 8:9; 1 Corinthians 10:31.

What day is the Sabbath, Saturday or Sunday?

Short answer: under the Old Covenant the Sabbath was the seventh day (Saturday), but historic Christian practice honors the first day (Sunday), the Lord’s Day, in light of Christ’s resurrection. Genesis 2:2-3; Exodus 20:8-11; Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2; Revelation 1:10 (all BSB).

One opposing historic view: Seventh-day Sabbatarians insist the fourth commandment remains a perpetual, literal seventh‑day obligation (Saturday) for Christians, appealing to Exodus 20:8-11 (BSB).

My position (Reformed Baptist/Christian): the Sabbath principle—God’s gift of rest for his people—is fulfilled in Christ and continues as a pattern of Sabbath rest and weekly worship, while the New Testament shows Christians gathering on the first day to remember the resurrection (Hebrews 4:9-10; Colossians 2:16-17; Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2; Romans 14:5, all BSB). Practically, worship with your local faithful church on the Lord’s Day (Sunday) and set aside habitual work that day for rest, prayer, and hearing the Word; if you struggle over this, talk with your pastor and I can point you to short readings for a Lord’s Day devotion. Why did God punish Pharaoh for Abram’s lie (Genesis 12:17)?

Short answer: God punished Pharaoh because Pharaoh sinned in taking Abram’s wife into his house, and because God, in his sovereign holiness and covenant faithfulness, intervened to protect Abraham, Sarah, and the promise through Abraham. Scripture holds both men accountable—Abram for his lie and Pharaoh for the moral wrong he committed—while God preserves the line through which his covenant blessing will come.

Explanation: Abram’s deception (he said Sarai was his sister) put Sarah at risk (Genesis 12:10-13). Pharaoh then took her into his house, which made him guilty of wrongdoing against another man’s wife and exposed Sarah to sexual and social danger; God brought affliction on Pharaoh’s house until Sarah was returned (Genesis 12:17; Genesis 12:18-20). God’s action shows both his moral hatred of sexual/marital violation and his providential care for the covenant promises given to Abraham (the promise that through him all nations would be blessed, Genesis 12:3). Note also that Abraham’s fear and deceit were wrong—God later rebukes and prevents a similar episode with Abimelech (Genesis 20:3-7)—so God judges evil without excusing Abram’s sin.

Theological significance in concise terms: this episode displays God’s holiness and justice (he punishes sin), his providence in protecting the covenant seed, and his mercy in rescuing Sarah and Abraham despite Abraham’s failing—pointing to the biblical themes of covenant faithfulness, judgment, and grace.

Practical next step: read Genesis 12:10-20 and Genesis 20:1-7 in the BSB, pray and ask God to grow your trust in him so you don’t resort to fear-driven sin, and consider talking with a pastor or mature believer about confessing and growing in truthfulness. Christmas Story — Day 4 — Do Christians worship Mary?

Short answer: No. Christians worship the triune God alone; to worship Mary as though she were divine or a second mediator would be idolatry. Exodus 20:3-5; Matthew 4:10.

What the Bible shows, and why we honor Mary differently than we worship God:
- Scripture honors Mary as the mother of the Lord and a model of faith—see her song of praise in Luke 1:46-55—yet every biblical act of worship is directed to God and to Jesus, not to Mary. Luke 1:46-55; Hebrews 12:2.
- The New Testament teaches that Jesus is the only mediator between God and people, so prayer and worship rightly come to God through Christ alone. John 14:6; 1 Timothy 2:5.

How historic Christians have handled this:
- Roman Catholic teaching distinguishes latria (worship, reserved for God), dulia (veneration of the saints), and hyperdulia (a special honor for Mary) as a way to avoid idolatry while showing high honor to Mary.  
- My position: We may and should honor Mary as the mother of Jesus and praise her faith, but we must not worship her or pray to her as if she were a mediator alongside Christ, because the gospel centers on Christ’s unique atoning work and mediatorship. 1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 12:2.

A practical next step: Read Luke 1 and 1 Timothy 2:1-6 in the Berean Standard Bible, and in your next time of prayer practice addressing God the Father through Jesus (for example, brief prayer: thank Jesus for his saving work and ask the Father for help). What is Kemetic spirituality / Kemetism?

Short definition: Kemetic spirituality or Kemetism is a modern revival and reconstruction of the religion and spiritual practices of ancient Egypt (Kemet), rooted in devotion to the Egyptian gods and goddesses, an ethic centered on Maʽat (order, truth, justice), ritual worship, and often ancestor veneration.

Key points, briefly
- Name and aim: "Kemet" is the ancient Egyptian name for the land; modern Kemetism seeks to recover temple rites, prayers, festivals, and moral teachings from ancient texts and archaeology.
- Beliefs: typically polytheistic (honoring deities such as Amun‑Ra, Isis, Osiris, Horus, Anubis), with emphasis on Maʽat, cycles of death and rebirth in myth, and a worldview where divine forces are actively present.
- Practice: daily devotions, offerings, ritual prayers, observance of ancient festivals, use of hymns and spells, and sometimes magic (heka) and medium/ancestor practices. Some groups reconstruct rituals from inscriptional sources; others blend ancient elements with modern pagan or Afrocentric spirituality.
- Diversity and historicity: communities range from careful academic reconstructionists (trying to follow ancient forms closely) to eclectic neo‑pagan groups; because much of ancient practice must be inferred from fragmentary evidence, modern expressions vary and are partly contemporary creations.

Why people are drawn to it
- Cultural and ancestral identity, a desire for embodied ritual and community, fascination with ancient Egypt, and the moral ideal of Maʽat are common attractions.

A Christian perspective (short and pastoral)
- Christians respect sincere spiritual seeking, but the Bible teaches that worship belongs to the one true God and warns against idolatry (Exodus 20:3-4; Isaiah 44:6-20). Christians confess Jesus Christ as the sole mediator and way to God (John 14:6; Acts 4:12). If you are exploring these things and want to think biblically about them, talk with a trusted pastor and read the Gospel accounts to compare claims and ultimate hope.

If you want to go deeper
- Read both scholarly overviews of ancient Egyptian religion and first‑hand translations (inscriptions, Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, Book of the Dead) and visit or talk with contemporary Kemetic practitioners to hear how they live it today. If you’re exploring spiritually, one next step is to pray and discuss what you learn with a mature Christian you trust.
Should Christians be concerned about the idea of Sharia Law?

Short answer: Yes—Christians should be informed and discerning about Sharia law, but the degree and manner of concern depend on what is meant by “Sharia” and the local context.

What Sharia is, briefly: Sharia is a broad term for Islamic teachings about worship, morality, family life, and in some interpretations, public law, drawn from the Quran and the Hadith. In practice it ranges from private religious guidance for Muslim believers to formal legal codes or criminal penalties in some states. Concern is different when Sharia is private religious practice, versus when it is imposed by the state as coercive public law.

Why this matters biblically: The Bible teaches respect for governing authorities (Romans 13:1-7; 1 Peter 2:13-17), yet it also teaches that Christians must obey God rather than men when the two conflict (Acts 5:29). We are commanded to love our neighbor and witness winsomely (Matthew 22:37-39; 1 Peter 3:15). So a Christian response must protect conscience and religious liberty without giving way to fear, hatred, or injustice.

Two common Christian responses, briefly:
- Concern/defense view in one sentence: Some Christians rightly oppose any legal system that would coerce Christians, deny religious freedom, or punish belief and seek to defend civil liberties and the rule of law.
- Relational/witness view in one sentence: Other Christians emphasize relationship-building and gospel witness to Muslim neighbors, warning against fear and stereotyping and urging Christians to show Christlike love while engaging civic issues prudently.

My position in one sentence and why: Christians should be watchful and oppose coercive imposition of Sharia where it violates religious freedom or basic human rights, while also refusing to descend into xenophobia, pursuing both faithful civic advocacy and patient gospel witness, because Scripture calls us to both lawful submission and loving, courageous testimony (Romans 13:1-7; Acts 5:29; Matthew 22:37-39).

Practical next steps (concrete, actionable):
- Learn the distinctions: read reliable descriptions of Sharia (private practice vs. state law) and the specific proposals or policies in your locality before reacting.  
- Pray and ask your church elders for guidance, and encourage your congregation to learn how to love Muslim neighbors without compromising truth (pray for wisdom, for boldness to witness, and for protection where needed).  
- If you are worried about public policy, engage civically: contact representatives, support organizations that protect religious liberty, and advocate for equal protection under the law.  
- Build relationships: serve and speak the gospel to Muslim neighbors with humility and gentleness, while standing for conscience rights.

A short pastoral word: It is right to be concerned about threats to religious freedom, but do not let fear harden you—Christ calls you to both courageous truth-telling and compassionate love (1 Peter 3:15; Matthew 22:37-39). If you are personally facing legal or safety threats because of your faith, contact your pastor or a trusted Christian legal/counseling resource immediately.

If you want, tell me whether you mean Sharia in your country, in news reports, or as a theological question, and I will give more specific guidance or resources.
What is the meaning of the foxes in Song of Solomon 2:15?

Brief answer: In Song of Solomon 2:15 the "foxes" are a poetic metaphor for small, secret faults or temptations that, left unchecked, will spoil a blossoming love or the fruitfulness of a relationship.

Two historic readings in one sentence each: Some readers take the image more narrowly as literal foxes that damage vineyards in the ancient Near East; others take it allegorically, seeing the foxes as small sins, cares, or jealousies that undermine marital affection and devotion.  

My position and why: I favor the allegorical/metaphorical reading because the book is written as love poetry using agricultural imagery (vineyards, blossoms) and the verbatim warning to "catch the foxes" fits a moral and pastoral concern for guarding the love between the lovers; the agricultural threat (real foxes) serves as a vivid image for the moral danger. See Song of Solomon 2:15 BSB.

Theological and pastoral significance: The verse calls for vigilance in the life of love and sanctification, warning that what seems minor can corrupt what is flourishing, which ties to biblical calls to guard the heart and relationship (Proverbs 4:23 BSB) and to love and protect one another in marriage (Ephesians 5:25–33 BSB). Small, unconfessed sins or petty resentments function like the little foxes, chipping away at intimacy and witness.

Practical next step: Ask the Lord to show you one "little fox" in your heart or relationship, write it down, confess it to God and to a trusted spouse or accountability partner, and put one concrete guard in place this week (for example, remove a tempting opportunity, set a time for honest conversation, or begin a short daily Scripture-and-prayer habit). If you want, tell me whether you mean this as a literary question or because of a personal situation, and I can help you apply it more specifically.
What is the false cause fallacy?

The false cause fallacy is an informal logical error where someone assumes that because two things occur together or one follows the other, the first caused the second, without sufficient evidence for a causal link.

Common forms:
- Post hoc ergo propter hoc, “after this, therefore because of this” (A happened, then B happened, so A caused B).  
- Confusing correlation with causation (two variables move together but are both caused by something else).  
- Reversing cause and effect (claiming A causes B when B actually causes A).

How to spot and avoid it:
- Ask for evidence of a mechanism showing how A would produce B.  
- Look for alternative explanations and confounding variables.  
- Check whether the claim survives stronger tests (experiments, controlled studies, or consistent replication).  
- Ask, “How do you know this caused that?” and require more than timing or mere association.

Biblical relevance: Christians are called to careful discernment and to test claims rather than accept them uncritically, so avoid rash conclusions and seek truth (Proverbs 18:13; Proverbs 18:17; 1 Thessalonians 5:21).

Practical next step: the next time you hear a causal claim, pause and ask for the evidence, ask whether other factors could explain the link, and look for independent studies or counterexamples; if you want, tell me a specific claim and I’ll help evaluate it.
Is it a sin to share copyrighted material on the internet?

Short answer: generally, yes—knowingly posting or distributing copyrighted work without the owner’s permission is a form of stealing and dishonesty and so is sinful, though sharing what is legitimately public domain, licensed for sharing, or covered by lawful fair use is not sinful.

Why: the Bible forbids stealing (Exodus 20:15 BSB) and calls Christians away from dishonest gain toward honest work and generosity (Ephesians 4:28 BSB). Christians are also called to submit to governing authorities and laws (Romans 13:1-7 BSB) and to love our neighbor, which includes respecting their property and livelihood (Matthew 22:37-39 BSB). When we harm someone’s rightful income we should make it right, as Zacchaeus did by offering restitution (Luke 19:8 BSB).

Practical steps
- Stop sharing infringing files, remove uploads, and replace them with legal links or public-domain/Creative Commons versions.
- If you have profited or caused loss, consider contacting the creator, ask how to make restitution, and compensate them if needed.
- When in doubt, seek permission or use licensed sources (streaming services, publishers, official channels) or materials explicitly labeled for reuse.

Pastoral note: if you’ve done this, repent to God and, where possible, seek to restore what was harmed (1 John 1:9 BSB; Luke 19:8 BSB). Repentance means turning from the sin and trusting Christ’s forgiveness and power to change. If you’d like, tell me a bit about the situation (what you shared, whether it was intentional), and I can suggest concrete next steps for making amends.
What does the Bible say about Christian fathers?

Briefly: The Bible calls Christian fathers to reflect God’s fatherly character by leading sacrificially, teaching and discipling their children, providing and protecting, and disciplining in love, all while depending on Christ and the Spirit for their own growth (sanctification).

Key biblical emphases (with passages):
- Reflect God’s compassion and mercy as your model, because the Lord shows compassion like a father (Psalm 103:13 BSB).  
- Servant headship and sacrificial love: husbands/fathers are given leadership in the home but called to love sacrificially as Christ loved the church (Ephesians 5:23-25 BSB; 1 Peter 3:7 BSB).  
- Teach and disciple daily: instruct your children in God’s Word in ordinary life—talking about it at home, on the road, at bedtime (Deuteronomy 6:6-7 BSB; Proverbs 22:6 BSB; Ephesians 6:4 BSB).  
- Discipline in love and for growth: God uses loving parental correction to train children toward righteousness (Proverbs 13:24 BSB; Hebrews 12:7-11 BSB).  
- Provide and care practically for your household (1 Timothy 5:8 BSB), and protect their spiritual welfare (Psalm 127:3-5 BSB).  
- Be an example of holiness and repentance: children learn by imitation, so demonstrate a life shaped by Scripture, prayer, and humility (Titus 2:7-8 BSB; Ephesians 5:1 BSB).  
- Depend on God for wisdom and strength, asking him and coming boldly to the throne of grace (James 1:5 BSB; Hebrews 4:16 BSB).

Practical, concrete applications:
- Start a simple, regular family practice: 10 minutes of Scripture reading, one short comment, and a prayer each evening (use Deuteronomy 6:6-7 BSB and a child-appropriate passage).  
- If you struggle with anger, inconsistency, or absence, confess that to your family and a trusted elder, ask forgiveness, and set one measurable change this month (for example, one uninterrupted hour with each child weekly). Hebrews 12:11 BSB reminds us discipline produces a harvest of righteousness.

A pastoral word: If you’ve failed or feel inadequate, God’s grace covers failure and fuels growth—repent, receive his forgiveness, and rely on the Spirit to change you (Hebrews 4:16 BSB; 1 Peter 5:10 BSB). If you want, tell me whether you’re a new father, seasoned father, or preparing to be one, and I’ll suggest a short family devotion or one practical first step tailored to your situation.
What was the process of deciding on the Old Testament canon?

Brief answer: The Old Testament canon was not decided by a single vote but recognized gradually—first by Jewish communities (the Torah, then the Prophets, then the Writings) according to tests like prophetic/authorship, antiquity, liturgical use, and theological consistency, and later affirmed by the Christian church while debating which Septuagint books to accept.

What that looked like in history (concise):
- Jewish formation, Second Temple era: The Pentateuch (Torah) was accepted earliest; the Prophets (Neviim) were widely recognized by the late Second Temple period; the Writings (Ketuvim) reached broader acceptance later, so the Hebrew canon took shape over several centuries in a Jewish context (Second Temple Judaism).  
- The Septuagint (a Greek translation of Hebrew Scriptures used by Hellenistic Jews) circulated widely and was the OT for many early Christians, which created a broader list of books in some communities.  
- Early Christian usage, appeals to apostolic and prophetic authority, and later church councils (together with longstanding liturgical practice) consolidated which books churches read as Scripture, but differences remained—hence Protestant, Catholic, and Orthodox differences about the deuterocanonical/apocryphal books.

Key theological points:
- The church’s criterion for recognizing Scripture was rooted in the conviction that God’s Word is inspired and authoritative (2 Timothy 3:16 BSB), and Jesus and the apostles treated the Hebrew Scriptures as Scripture (Luke 24:44 BSB).  
- The process is therefore a recognition (not invention) of which writings are God-breathed and authoritative—a matter of canon and inspiration, judged by historic trustworthiness, prophetic or apostolic authority, and faithful use in worship and teaching.

Two historic positions in one sentence each:
- Many churches (Catholic and Orthodox) accept the larger Septuagint collection (including deuterocanonical books) because of long-standing liturgical use and early church acceptance.  
- Protestants follow the Hebrew canon (the books of the Jewish Bible) and exclude the deuterocanonical books, stressing the Hebrew textual tradition and the canon the New Testament writers most often quote.

My position and why (brief): I hold to the Hebrew Old Testament canon (the Protestant list) because the church should submit to Scripture alone (sola Scriptura), and the Hebrew canon corresponds most closely to the Scriptures Jesus and the apostles appealed to and to the Jewish canon that undergirds the New Testament testimony (Luke 24:44; 2 Timothy 3:16 BSB).

Practical next step: If you want to see the differences for yourself, compare a Protestant OT table of contents with a Catholic one and read Luke 24:44 and 2 Timothy 3:16 this week, then talk with a pastor or join a Bible study to discuss how the canon affects how we read and apply Scripture.
Is cyber-sex / phone sex a sin?

Short answer: Yes—sexualized conversations or acts by phone, text, video, or online with someone who is not your spouse are sinful, because Scripture treats such sexual activity and lust as sexual immorality and adultery of the heart. Jesus warns that looking with lust is equivalent to committing adultery in the heart, Matthew 5:27-28 BSB; Christians are called to flee sexual immorality and honor the body as the Lord’s, 1 Corinthians 6:18-20 BSB.

A little more detail and pastoral help:
- If you are single, erotic sexual contact (including cyber-sex or phone sex) is fornication—sin against God’s design for sexual intimacy to be within marriage; see 1 Thessalonians 4:3-5 BSB.  
- If you are married, sexual activity with someone other than your spouse is adultery; even sexual arousal with others is a violation of Christ’s command to purity, Matthew 5:27-28 BSB; Hebrews 13:4 BSB. Sexual intimacy with your spouse, by contrast, is a God‑ordained good when exercised within marriage, 1 Corinthians 7:3-5 BSB.  
- Digital sexual activity also creates a lasting, risky trail (images/messages) that often increases temptation and harms marriages and reputations—so prudence and boundaries are essential.

What to do next (one practical step): stop the contact immediately and put a concrete barrier in place (for example, delete the app/contact, change passwords, and set an accountability partner or app). Then confess this to a trusted Christian leader or mature friend and walk with them through repentance and accountability, James 5:16 BSB; Galatians 6:1 BSB.

Gospel hope: all of us fall short and need Christ’s forgiveness; the Bible teaches our sin separates us from God, Romans 3:23 BSB, but Christ died for sinners and rose so we can be forgiven and renewed, Romans 5:8 BSB; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 BSB. Turn (repent) from this sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new power to live in holiness.

Do unbelievers immediately go to hell when they die?

Short answer: Historic orthodox Christianity teaches that unbelievers undergo a particular (immediate) judgment at death and enter conscious punishment in the intermediate state, awaiting the final resurrection and judgment; a minority of Christians instead teach “soul sleep,” that the dead are unconscious until the resurrection.

- Direct answer in one sentence: Unbelievers, at death, face a particular judgment and enter conscious suffering (often called Hades or hell) in the intermediate state before the final resurrection and condemnation.  
- Best opposing historic view in one sentence: The soul-sleep view says the dead are unconscious, experiencing no conscious reward or punishment until the general resurrection and final judgment.  
- My position and why (brief): I hold to particular judgment and conscious existence after death because the New Testament presents believers as immediately “with the Lord” when absent from the body (2 Corinthians 5:8), Paul longs to “depart and be with Christ” as better by immediate presence (Philippians 1:23), and Jesus’ parable of the rich man and Lazarus depicts conscious awareness after death (Luke 16:19-31); Scripture also affirms that death is followed by judgment (Hebrews 9:27) and that there will be a final resurrection to life or judgment (John 5:28-29; Matthew 25:41-46). For the reality of conscious souls waiting for final vindication, see Revelation 6:9-10.

Pastoral note and practical step: If this raises anxiety about you or a loved one, first pray and ask God for clarity, then speak with a mature pastor or elder in a Bible-believing church and read the Gospel of John and 1 Corinthians 15 to see the Gospel’s promise more fully; if you are uncertain about your own standing before God, turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for your forgiveness and new life.

Would you like me to explain how particular judgment and the final judgment differ, or to walk through the key texts more slowly?
How should Christians react to persecution against the LGBTQ community?

Christians should oppose and refuse persecution, protect and care for those who are harmed, and speak the truth of the Gospel with compassion and humility. We must not answer sin with violence or hatred, and we must not abandon clear biblical teaching about sexual ethics; both justice and holiness are commanded by Scripture.

Why, biblically:
- Every person bears the image of God, so even those who sin deserve dignity and protection, Genesis 1:27.  
- Jesus taught the two greatest commands, to love God and love your neighbor, Matthew 22:37-40, and to love enemies and pray for persecutors, Matthew 5:44.  
- God calls his people to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly, Micah 6:8, and to show no partiality, James 2:1-9.  
- We are to repay no one evil for evil and to leave judgment with the Lord, Romans 12:17-21.  
- At the same time, Scripture teaches that sexual sin is real and calls people to repentance and new life in Christ, Romans 1:24-27; 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, and the church is to restore gently, Galatians 6:1.  
- Christians are to be ready to give a reasoned witness with gentleness and respect, 1 Peter 3:15, and to honor all people, 1 Peter 2:17.

Concrete, practical ways Christians should respond (actionable steps):
1. Oppose violence and dehumanization, and support safety for victims, contacting authorities when someone is in immediate danger. (Justice, Romans 12:17-21.)  
2. Offer practical compassion—listen, provide shelter, medical care, legal help, or referrals—so persecuted people are not abandoned. (Love your neighbor, Matthew 22:37-40.)  
3. Speak the truth in love: name sin biblically but without contempt, seek restoration rather than condemnation, and share the Gospel as the only hope for forgiveness and renewal. (Ephesians 4:15; Galatians 6:1.)  
4. Advocate for fair, nonviolent civil protections that guard basic human dignity and safety while maintaining conviction about sexual ethics; seek wise, faith-consistent public engagement rather than either silence or hatred. (Micah 6:8; James 2:1-9.)  
5. Equip your local church to receive and care for those hurting, to disciple sinners toward sanctification, and to practice hospitality without compromising biblical truth. (1 Corinthians 6:11; Galatians 6:1.)  
6. Pray, repent of any hatred or indifference in your own heart, and ask God for wisdom in how best to love and witness, Philippians 4:6-7; James 5:16.

A pastoral note about truth and grace: calling sin by its biblical name does not justify cruelty. True holiness pursues justice and mercy together, reflecting the imago Dei in every person while calling everyone to repent and trust Jesus for forgiveness and new life. The Gospel is the only good news for sinners: turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for forgiveness and new life.

If you want, I can help you draft a short church statement responding to a local incident, or give specific pastoral steps for caring for someone who has been persecuted. Which would be most helpful?
How did Satan and other angels sin if heaven is sinless?

Short answer: God created the angels holy, but like human beings they were given moral freedom and some of them exercised that freedom in willful rebellion, committing what Scripture calls the angelic fall and therefore being cast out of heaven. 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 1:6; Revelation 12:7-9.

A little explanation: the Bible describes certain angels as having “sinned” and being reserved for judgment (2 Peter 2:4), and it pictures a war in heaven where Satan and his angels are defeated and expelled from God’s presence (Revelation 12:7-9). Passages about the proud fall of a ruler (Isaiah 14:12-15; Ezekiel 28:12-17) are principally addressed to earthly kings, but the prophetic language has long been understood as reflecting the pride and overthrow of the heavenly rebel, showing that pride and self-exaltation led to that rebellion. The theological points are: angels were created good, they possess intellect and will, some chose rebellion (angelic rebellion), and God’s holiness and justice rightly judged that rebellion.

Pastoral application: guard against pride and the temptation to self-exalt—humble dependence on Christ is the biblical cure (see 1 Peter 5:6-9). If you’d like, read Revelation 12 and 2 Peter 2 together and I can help walk through the passages step by step.
What is the greatest sin?

Short answer in one sentence: The Bible identifies blasphemy against the Holy Spirit—final, willful, settled rejection of God’s saving work in Christ—as the uniquely unforgivable or greatest sin, because it refuses the only means of forgiveness (Matthew 12:31-32; Mark 3:28-30; Luke 12:10).

Historic Christian alternatives in one sentence: Historically some Christians stress pride as the root and therefore the greatest sin (the heart’s exchange of God for self), while others say plain unbelief or rejection of Christ is the gravest offense because it closes the door to salvation (Romans 1:21-23; John 3:18).

My position and why, in one clear sentence: I hold that the gravest sin is final impenitence—that persistent, willful refusal to repent and trust Christ—because forgiveness is the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit, and Scripture treats a settled rejection of that saving work as unforgiven while always calling repentant sinners to receive mercy (Hebrews 10:26-29; John 3:16; 1 Peter 3:18; 1 John 1:9).

A few pastoral applications and a concrete next step:
- If you fear you have committed this sin, take heart in the Bible’s clarity that a repentant, concerned heart is not the final impenitence it describes; Scripture calls sinners to turn and be forgiven (Acts 3:19; 1 John 1:9).  
- The practical next step is to turn (repent) and trust Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life, seek a faithful local church, and speak with a pastor or mature believer for counsel and accountability (John 14:6; Romans 6:23; Acts 3:19).

What does the Bible say about sedition?

Short answer: The Bible treats sedition—violent or unlawful rebellion against civil authority—as sinful because civil authorities are ordained by God, though it also affirms that when human rulers command disobedience to God, Christians must obey God instead. Key texts teach both respect for government and the priority of obedience to God.

Scripture and how it applies
- God ordains governing authorities and calls believers to submit to them, because they serve God’s purposes for order and justice, not merely human preference, Romans 13:1-7; 1 Peter 2:13-17.  
- Christians should render lawful obligations (taxes, respect) to the state, Matthew 22:21.  
- The New Testament allows principled refusal when rulers demand disobedience to God: “We must obey God rather than men,” Acts 5:29 (see also the apostles’ courageous witness in Acts 4).  
- The Bible records examples of rebellion and its consequences: Absalom’s uprising against David (2 Samuel 15–18) and the criminal described as an insurrectionist, Barabbas (Mark 15:7; Luke 23:19), showing God’s disapproval of violent revolt and the human cost of sedition.  
- The Lord sovereignly judges nations and rebellions; Psalm 2 contrasts human plotting with God’s sovereign rule and warns against opposing him.

Theological summary
- The Bible grounds civil government in God’s providence (divine ordinance) and labels unlawful rebellion as sinful because it attacks God’s means of maintaining order and justice; yet the higher law of God governs conscience, so obedience to God supersedes obedience to sinful human commands (this is the basis for biblical civil disobedience, not lawless revolt).

Practical guidance (one concrete step)
- If you’re wrestling with a political or moral conflict, read Romans 13:1-7 and Acts 5:29 this week, pray for wisdom and your leaders, and bring the situation to your pastor or elders before taking public action; they can help you discern whether a faithful, nonviolent course of civil disobedience (if required by conscience) is appropriate and wise.

If you’re under persecution or fear for safety, tell a trusted church leader and seek legal or humanitarian help; Scripture calls the church to care for those who suffer (Psalm 37; Hebrews 13:3).

If you’d like, I can summarize Romans 13 and Acts 5 in a few sentences, or help you draft a biblically rooted plan for how to respond to a specific unjust law or political situation.
What is the International Christian Church (ICC), and what do they believe?

Do you mean the International Christian Church (often called the ICC) led by Kip McKean, or the International Churches of Christ (ICOC), which is the earlier global movement out of which the ICC later emerged? They are related but distinct; which one would you like me to describe in detail?

Quick differences so you can choose:
- International Christian Church (Kip McKean): a network formed after McKean’s departure from the ICOC that emphasizes aggressive evangelism, close discipleship/accountability, church planting, and believer’s baptism as the entry into the church; it is often described as having strong centralized leadership and a discipling structure.  
- International Churches of Christ (ICOC): the larger movement that grew from McKean’s earlier work within the Churches of Christ tradition, which likewise stressed discipleship, baptism, small-group accountability, and rapid expansion, but which underwent internal reforms and controversy in the 2000s.

Do some Christmas traditions have pagan origins?

Short answer: Yes—many popular Christmas customs (for example, celebrating near the winter solstice, evergreen decorations, certain feasting customs) have roots in pre‑Christian festivals and folk practices, but whether Christians may use them is a non‑essential and a matter of conscience and discernment.

Two historic Christian responses, fairly stated:
- Some Christians avoid traditions with pagan origins, fearing syncretism and wanting to keep worship pure from practices tied to false religion (see Deuteronomy 12:29-32; Colossians 2:8).
- Other Christians receive and redeem neutral cultural customs, intentionally re‑casting them to point to Christ and the gospel rather than the original pagan meanings (see Acts 17:22-23; Romans 14:5-6).

My position in one sentence: It is reasonable and faithful to reject any practice that promotes idolatry or false worship, while freely using and Christianizing otherwise neutral cultural customs so they glorify Christ—do all things for the glory of God (1 Corinthians 10:31) and avoid being taken captive by empty human tradition (Colossians 2:8).

Practical application: Before adopting or keeping a custom, ask (1) does this promote or involve worship of anything besides Christ? (2) does it point my family and guests toward the gospel? If the answer to (1) is yes, discard it; if the answer to (2) is no, consider how to reframe the practice to teach about Christ (for example, use the Advent wreath or a nativity reading to explain Jesus’ coming). For help with a specific tradition (Christmas tree, December 25, Yule, mistletoe, etc.), tell me which one and I’ll review its history and a gospel‑centered way to handle it.

Supporting passages: Deuteronomy 12:29-32; Colossians 2:8; Acts 17:22-23; Romans 14:5-6; 1 Corinthians 10:31; Luke 2:10-11.
Where does the Old Testament predict the coming of Christ?

Short answer: The Old Testament repeatedly predicts the coming Messiah, promising a promised seed, a Davidic king, a suffering servant who dies for sin, and a risen/priestly Lord. Key passages include the following.

- The first promise of a Savior, the seed who will crush the serpent, Genesis 3:15.  
- The promise to Abraham that all nations will be blessed in his seed, Genesis 12:3; Genesis 22:18.  
- The Davidic king promised to build an eternal throne, 2 Samuel 7:12-16; Jeremiah 23:5-6.  
- A prophet like Moses who will speak God’s word, Deuteronomy 18:15-19.  
- Royal and priestly messianic texts, Psalm 2 (the Lord’s Anointed) and Psalm 110 (a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek).  
- Suffering and atonement language anticipating substitutionary death, Psalm 22; Isaiah 53:1-12.  
- Promise of bodily vindication and life, Psalm 16:10 (used in the New Testament for resurrection).  
- Birthplace and family line, Micah 5:2; the virgin birth prophecy, Isaiah 7:14.  
- Predictions of the Messiah’s reign and titles, Isaiah 9:6-7; Daniel 7:13-14 (the Son of Man given everlasting rule).  
- Prophecies of the manner and ministry: Zechariah 9:9 (humble king), Zechariah 12:10 (they will look on him whom they pierced).  
- Messianic forerunner and timing, Malachi 3:1; the “sign of Jonah” type in Jonah 1:17 and the Passover/Passover-lamb type in Exodus 12.  
- The sacrificial system and Day of Atonement as types that point to atonement in Christ, Leviticus 16; Exodus 12.

(Representative citations: Genesis 3:15; Genesis 12:3; Genesis 22:18; Deuteronomy 18:15-19; 2 Samuel 7:12-16; Jeremiah 23:5-6; Psalm 2:1-12; Psalm 22:1-31; Psalm 16:10; Psalm 110:1-4; Isaiah 7:14; Isaiah 9:6-7; Isaiah 53:1-12; Isaiah 61:1-3; Micah 5:2; Daniel 7:13-14; Zechariah 9:9; Zechariah 12:10; Malachi 3:1; Exodus 12; Leviticus 16.)

Theological significance in one sentence: together these passages point to the incarnate Messiah who is both Davidic King and suffering Servant, whose substitutionary death and bodily resurrection accomplish atonement and fulfill God’s covenant promises (incarnation, hypostatic union, penal substitution, resurrection).

Practical next step: read Isaiah 53, Psalm 22, and Psalm 110 this week, asking God to show you Jesus as you read, and then bring any questions to a pastor or a trusted Bible study leader. What is spiritual adultery?

Briefly: spiritual adultery is the Bible’s way of describing covenant unfaithfulness to God—when people turn from the Lord to rival “lovers” (idols such as money, pleasure, approval, power, or a life shaped by the world) and so break their relationship with him. Hosea and the prophets use the language of marriage to show how Israel (and by extension any believer or church) commits this sin (Hosea 2:2; James 4:4).

Biblical basis and meaning, in brief
- The Old Testament prophets repeatedly call Israel an unfaithful wife to illustrate idolatry and covenant betrayal (Hosea 2:2; Ezekiel 16:15-17; Jeremiah 3:8).  
- The New Testament keeps that imagery when warning the church about friendship with the world and false devotion (James 4:4; Revelation 17:1-6).  
The theological terms here are idolatry, covenant unfaithfulness, and apostasy—spiritual adultery is not merely moral failure but a breach of the covenant bond between God and his people. Ephesians pictures the church as Christ’s bride to underscore how serious that unfaithfulness is and how costly Christ’s rescue was (Ephesians 5:25-27).

What God does about it
- God both condemns and calls to repentance; the gospel is the remedy. Christ’s substitutionary death and resurrection deal with our unfaithfulness so we can be forgiven and restored (2 Corinthians 5:21; Romans 5:8). The call is to turn back to him and be renewed.

A pastoral, practical next step
- Ask God to show you any competing “lovers” in your heart, confess them specifically, and bring that to a mature Christian (pastor, elder, or trusted believer) this week for prayer and accountability. That concrete step helps move from confession to real change.

A simple gospel invitation
- If you recognize your own unfaithfulness, turn (repent) from that sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for your forgiveness and new life.

Would you like help identifying common idols or a few Scripture passages to read this week as you examine your heart?
How can we rely on the power of God?

Short answer: We rely on the power of God by depending on Christ and the promised Holy Spirit, using the means of grace (Scripture, prayer, fellowship, and the ordinances), confessing our weakness, and obeying in faith so God’s strength is displayed in our weakness. Ephesians 3:16-19; Acts 1:8; John 15:5

Why this is true (biblical basis)
- God has raised and exalted Christ and supplies power to his people (Ephesians 1:19-20; Philippians 4:13). 
- The Holy Spirit now dwells in believers to enable holy living and witness (Romans 8:11; Acts 1:8). 
- God’s common pattern is to make his power perfect in human weakness—so we are to boast in weakness and lean on his grace (2 Corinthians 12:9-10; Isaiah 40:29-31). 
- We access that power through the means of grace—abiding in Christ, approaching God with confidence, reading and obeying Scripture, and living in Christian community (John 15:5; Hebrews 4:16; Galatians 2:20).

Key theological terms: sanctification (the Spirit’s work making us holy), substitutionary atonement (Christ’s work that grounds our acceptance), and perseverance (God keeping his people).

Practical, concrete steps you can take this week
1) Pick one real weakness or need (for example: anger, fear, temptation, loneliness). Tell a trusted Christian and ask them to pray with you. James 5:16  
2) Each morning for seven days, spend five minutes: confess your weakness, ask the Spirit specifically for power to do the next faithful thing, and read one short passage (John 15 or Romans 8). Hebrews 4:16; John 15:5  
3) Memorize Philippians 4:13 and quote it when you feel weak so your heart aligns with God’s truth. Philippians 4:13  
4) Take one immediate act of obedience in that area (call a person, avoid a trigger, do a loving task) and thank God for enabling you to obey. Galatians 2:20

A word of pastoral encouragement
God delights to strengthen those who depend on him; your weakness is not a final verdict but the very place where his grace and power meet you (2 Corinthians 12:9-10; Isaiah 40:31). If you want, tell me the specific area you’re asking about and I’ll suggest a short Scripture reading and one concrete step to take.
What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled the law, but did not abolish it?

Short answer: Jesus “fulfilled” the law by perfectly keeping its demands, accomplishing its prophetic and sacrificial purposes, and bearing its penalty for sinners, while He did not “abolish” the law’s moral authority—He taught that the law still reveals God’s holy standard (Matthew 5:17-18 BSB).

Two-sentence secondary-doctrine summary required by your question:
- Direct answer in one sentence: Jesus completed what the Mosaic law pointed to and required, and in doing so brought its types and penalties to their goal while maintaining the law’s moral witness (Matthew 5:17-18 BSB; Romans 8:3-4 BSB).
- Opposing one-sentence view: Some Christians argue that the entire Mosaic system—civil, ceremonial, and moral—is still binding on the church today.
- My position (Reformed Baptist, briefly): The moral law (for example, the Ten Commandments) continues as God’s rule for life, but the ceremonial and civil aspects of the Mosaic covenant were fulfilled in Christ and are no longer binding in the same way because Christ has inaugurated the new covenant and accomplished the once-for-all sacrifice the types pointed to (Hebrews 8:6-13 BSB; Hebrews 9:11-14 BSB).

How to think about “fulfilled” (three basic senses)
1) Legal and moral fulfillment: Jesus lived perfect obedience to God’s law on our behalf (often called his active obedience), so the righteous requirement of the law is fulfilled for those who are in him by the Spirit (Romans 8:3-4 BSB).  
2) Penal and atoning fulfillment: Jesus bore the penalty that sinners deserved, satisfying God’s justice so sinners are justified by faith through his death and resurrection (Romans 3:24-26 BSB; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 BSB if you want the Gospel summary).  
3) Typological and prophetic fulfillment: The Mosaic sacrifices, festivals, temple, and many prophetic promises were foreshadows that found their substance in Christ’s person and work (Matthew 5:17 BSB; Hebrews 9:11-14 BSB; Hebrews 8:6-13 BSB; Jeremiah 31:31-34 BSB).

What “did not abolish” means practically
- Jesus did not cancel the law’s moral truth; the law still shows God’s character, exposes sin, and points sinners to their need of Christ (Galatians 3:24-25 BSB; Romans 3:31 BSB).  
- But because Christ fulfilled the ceremonial system and established the new covenant, Christians are no longer bound to the Mosaic ceremonial observances and civil structures as they were under that covenant (Hebrews 8:6-13 BSB).

One practical application
- If you are a believer, ask the Holy Spirit to show you how the moral commands reveal Christ and shape your heart, and practice one concrete step this week (for example, choose one commandment to memorize and pray about, asking God to make it practical in a specific relationship).  
- If you are unsure about your standing before God, remember that the law shows our need; the Gospel is that Christ died and rose to take our penalty and give us his righteousness—turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life (Romans 3:24-26 BSB; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 BSB).

What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

The Major Prophets are the longer prophetic books: Isaiah, Jeremiah (with Lamentations commonly paired), Ezekiel, and Daniel. The Minor Prophets are the twelve shorter prophetic books, from Hosea through Malachi: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi.

"Major" and "Minor" describe length, not importance. These books record the prophetic office calling Israel to covenant faithfulness, announce God’s judgment and restoration, and contain many messianic and eschatological prophecies (for the prophet’s call, see Jeremiah 1:4-10 BSB; for messianic suffering and atonement, see Isaiah 53:4-6 BSB; for the Messiah’s birthplace, see Micah 5:2 BSB; for promise of the Spirit and future restoration, see Joel 2:28-29 BSB; for national restoration imagery, see Ezekiel 37:11-14 BSB).

A practical next step: try reading one Major Prophet (for example, Isaiah 1–12 this week) and one Minor Prophet (for example, Micah or Hosea), and ask your pastor or a study Bible about historical context and messianic connections. Would you like a short 4-week reading plan through selected Major and Minor Prophets?
Why believe in Jesus?

That is a weighty, honest question—thank you for asking. Briefly put: believe in Jesus because he is the God-man who dealt with our sin by his substitutionary death and confirmed his victory by rising bodily from the dead, and because through him God offers forgiveness and new life to anyone who trusts him.

Why that makes belief reasonable and life-changing
- We need it, because all people have sinned and fall short of God’s standard, and sin brings spiritual death, separation from God, and guilt, so we cannot save ourselves, no matter how moral we try to be, Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23.  
- Jesus did what we could not do, bearing our sin as our substitute, demonstrating God’s love, and securing justification by his death, Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 3:18. The historic gospel centers on his death, burial, and bodily resurrection, which the apostles insisted on and which the New Testament presents as the proof of God’s vindication, 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Acts 1:3.  
- The resurrection is decisive, because it shows Jesus is who he claimed to be, Lord and Savior, and opens the way to new life by the Spirit, 2 Corinthians 5:17; John 20:28. The Bible teaches that trusting Christ brings justification by faith and adoption into God’s family, doctrines central to historic Christian faith.

Common objections, briefly
- “How can a loving God allow suffering?” Christians believe God is good and sovereign, and that he is present with sufferers and will redeem suffering for ultimate good, even when we do not now see complete answers, Romans 8:28; 2 Corinthians 1:3-4.  
- “Is Christianity too exclusive?” The Bible teaches that Jesus is the only mediator who reconciles sinners to God, because he is both God and man, John 14:6; Acts 4:12. That claim is exclusive, but it is the gospel’s historic claim and offers a real remedy for sin and death.

A few concrete reasons people trust Jesus beyond abstract claims
- The testimony of eyewitnesses recorded in Scripture and the early church’s bold witness (Acts 1:3; Luke 1:1-4) give historical grounding.  
- The moral and spiritual fruit in believers’ lives—changed hearts, hope in suffering, courage to love sacrificially—points to real inward transformation, Galatians 2:20; 2 Corinthians 3:18.  
- Theologically, this is called the atonement and justification by faith, anchored in the hypostatic union, that is, Jesus being truly God and truly man.

A simple invitation
Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

One practical next step
If you want to explore further, read the Gospel of John slowly (start with John 1–3), ask God to show you the truth, and talk with a mature believer or pastor about what you read. If you’d like, I can suggest a short Scripture plan or help you think through the words of John 3:16 or Romans 10:9-10.

What is a Bible Baptist Church, and what do Bible Baptists believe?

A "Bible Baptist Church" is not a single denomination but a label used by Baptist congregations that strongly emphasize the Bible as the final authority for faith and practice, believer’s baptism by immersion, and conservative evangelical doctrine. In practice the term covers a range from independent “Bible Baptist” (often fundamentalist) churches to confessional Reformed Baptist congregations that share many core Baptist distinctives.

Brief summary of what Bible Baptists typically believe
- The Bible is God’s inspired, sufficient, and final authority for faith and life (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Psalm 119:160).  
- There is one God eternally existing in three persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19).  
- Jesus Christ is fully God and fully man, born of the Virgin Mary, died for sinners, was buried, and bodily rose again (John 1:14; Luke 1:35; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).  
- Salvation is by God’s grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:28).  
- Christ’s death is substitutionary and atoning for sin (penal substitution), accomplished on the cross (1 Peter 2:24; Isaiah 53:5).  
- The local church is the central visible expression of Christ’s body, governed by biblically qualified leaders (elders) and accountable to Scripture (Titus 1:5; Acts 20:28).  
- Baptism is for believers only and is performed by immersion as a public sign of union with Christ (Acts 2:41; Romans 6:3-4).  
- The Lord’s Supper is a commanded ordinance remembering Christ’s death and looking forward to His return (Luke 22:19-20).  
- Assurance and perseverance: those united to Christ are kept by His power (John 10:28-29).  
- Many Bible Baptist congregations affirm the inerrancy and clarity of Scripture and practice separation from theological error and often from churches they judge unfaithful to Scripture (2 Timothy 3:16-17).

Where Bible Baptists differ (short note on variety)
- “Bible Baptist” churches vary: some are independent, fundamentalist, and less confessional, while others are Reformed Baptist and confessional (for example shaped by the 1689 London Baptist Confession). The label does not guarantee identical beliefs on secondary or tertiary matters.

A specific secondary question often asked — spiritual gifts
- Direct answer: Reformed Bible Baptists typically hold that the extraordinary sign gifts (apostleship, prophecy as in the apostolic office, tongues as normative revelatory gift) have ceased, while ordinary gifts continue under the Holy Spirit’s sovereign work (cessationism).  
- Opposing historic Christian view: Continuationists say the miraculous and revelatory gifts continue today and remain a normative part of church life.  
- My position (Reformed Baptist): I hold the cessationist view because the New Testament links some gifts to the apostolic era and the foundation-laying work of the apostles, and because the sufficiency of Scripture makes ongoing foundational revelation unnecessary (Ephesians 2:20; 2 Timothy 3:16-17).

Practical next step
- If you want to evaluate a particular “Bible Baptist” church, ask for their written statement of faith, whether they practice believer’s baptism by immersion, how they describe the authority of Scripture, and whether they are confessional (for example, hold to the 1689 Confession). Then visit a service and speak with an elder or pastor about their beliefs and ministry.

If you’d like, tell me whether you’re asking about a specific congregation or region and I can help you evaluate that church’s statement of faith or recommend questions to ask the pastor.
What is sexual ethics?

Sexual ethics is the Bible-based moral teaching about human sexuality—what God designed sex for, what is forbidden, and how Christians are to live sexually holy in light of God’s character and covenant purposes.

Biblical foundations and core principles:
- Sex is created for the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman, pictured as a lifelong, exclusive union (Genesis 2:24; Matthew 19:4-6; Hebrews 13:4).  
- Marriage is a covenant that models Christ’s self-giving love for the church, giving sexual union its highest meaning (Ephesians 5:25-33).  
- The New Testament calls believers to flee sexual immorality and to pursue holiness because our bodies belong to Christ (1 Corinthians 6:18-20; 1 Thessalonians 4:3-5).  
- Sexual sins (fornication, adultery, lust, and related practices) are treated as real sin in Scripture and therefore require repentance and sanctification (use of the theological terms: sin, sanctification, covenant).

Pastoral and practical application:
- If you’re struggling, a concrete next step is to confess and repent, put accountable boundaries in place (filters, accountability partner, pastoral counsel), and grow in holiness through regular Scripture and prayer (for example, read Ephesians and 1 Corinthians with a trusted elder).  
- The gospel is central: all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory; Christ died and rose to forgive sinners and give new life, so turn (repent) from sexual sin and trust Jesus alone for forgiveness and transformation (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

What is spiritual sickness? What does it mean to be spiritually sick?

Briefly: spiritual sickness is the soul’s condition of being wounded, enslaved, or dead to God because of sin — a corruption of our affections and will that makes us alienated from God and unable to save ourselves. It shows up as unbelief, hardness of heart, indifference to God, continual sinful patterns, guilt, or a sense of being distant from God.

Why this is the Bible’s diagnosis: Scripture describes humanity as spiritually dead and alienated from God because of sin (Ephesians 2:1; Isaiah 59:2), with hearts prone to deceit and rebellion (Jeremiah 17:9). All people have sinned and fall short of God’s glory, so we need a remedy (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Jesus came to call sinners to repentance and to bring healing and new life (Matthew 9:12–13; John 3:16), and the gospel addresses the sickness by offering forgiveness, justification, and new life through faith in Christ (Romans 3:24; Acts 3:19).

Theological terms to name it: spiritual death, original sin (sinful nature), depravity (not meaning every person is as bad as possible, but that sin affects every part of us), alienation, justification (God declares the repentant sinner righteous), and sanctification (the ongoing healing and growth after conversion).

How it looks in daily life (signs of spiritual sickness):
- Little or no desire for God or Scripture (spiritual apathy).
- Repeated, unrepentant patterns of sinful behavior.
- A sense of guilt, shame, or inner emptiness that nothing worldly pleasures fill.
- Closedness to the claims of Christ or to gospel truth.

Good news and the remedy: Christ’s death and resurrection bring forgiveness and new life to the spiritually sick; we are called to repent and trust him who died and rose for us (John 3:16; Romans 6:23; Acts 3:19).

A practical next step: if this describes you, first confess briefly to God and ask him to help you repent, then read the Gospel of John (start with John 1–3) and talk with a pastor or a mature believer in a Bible-teaching church for counsel and prayer. If you’d like, I can suggest a short prayer outline to help you begin, or give a simple reading plan.

What is fellowship with God?

Fellowship with God is the real, personal communion (koinonia) that believers have with the triune God through union with Jesus Christ and the indwelling Holy Spirit—it is both a declared standing before God and an ongoing, intimate sharing of his life. 1 John 1:3-7; John 15:4-5; Romans 5:1.

What that looks like in practice
- A reconciled standing, so we have access to God by faith (justification and adoption): Romans 5:1; Ephesians 2:18.
- An ongoing walk of openness and obedience, where confession keeps fellowship flourishing and sin breaks it: 1 John 1:6-7; 1 John 1:9.
- Union with Christ and life by the Spirit, which produces growth and fruit as we abide in him: John 15:4-5; Romans 8:9-11.
- Visible expression in the body of Christ—Word, prayer, the Lord’s Supper, and mutual love with other believers: 1 Corinthians 10:16; Hebrews 10:24-25.

Concrete application
- Begin today by confessing any known sin, asking the Holy Spirit to renew affection for Christ, and spending 10–20 minutes reading the Gospel of John (to know Jesus) and 1 John (to learn how to walk in fellowship). Then share with a faithful Christian about what you sensed—this builds accountability and corporate fellowship. 1 John 1:9; John 15:4-5; Hebrews 10:24-25.

If you aren’t sure you have this fellowship
- The Bible teaches that all people are sinners in need of a Savior, and that God’s way into fellowship is through Christ’s atoning death and bodily resurrection; we receive that fellowship by repenting and trusting Jesus alone for forgiveness and new life. Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Turn (repent) from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What role does Iran play in the end times?

Short answer: The Bible names Persia (ancient Iran) among the nations aligned with “Gog” in Ezekiel’s prophecy of a last-days invasion of Israel, so many interpreters conclude modern Iran could be one of the players in a future coalition described in Ezekiel 38–39 (see Ezekiel 38:2–6; Ezekiel 38:14–16; Ezekiel 39:21–22).  

Opposing view in one sentence: Other careful interpreters warn against mapping ancient names directly onto current states or insist Ezekiel’s oracle is largely symbolic or primarily addressed to Ezekiel’s own era, so it does not require that contemporary Iran must play a role.  

My position and why (one sentence): I lean toward a future, largely literal fulfillment in which Persia (that is, the region corresponding to modern Iran) is named among Gog’s allies because the text explicitly lists Persia and Daniel likewise treats Persia as a distinct kingdom (see Daniel 8:20–22), but I urge caution about overconfident, detailed geopolitical predictions—Scripture’s main purpose in these chapters is to show God’s sovereign judgment and protection of His people, not to give a blow‑by‑blow news bulletin.  

Brief pastoral application: If you’re curious or anxious about this, read Ezekiel 38–39 and Daniel 8 slowly in a Bible (BSB), ask your pastor or a trusted teacher for guidance, pray for wisdom and for the nations, and remember to anchor your hope in Christ’s return and God’s faithfulness rather than the latest headlines (see Revelation 20:7–8; Revelation 16:16). If you’d like, I can summarize Ezekiel 38–39 verse by verse or recommend a short study plan.
What does Jesus mean when He says, “Verily, verily” or “Truly, truly,” in the gospels?

Brief answer: When Jesus says “Verily, verily” (literally the Greek amen, amen), he is using a double “amen” to solemnly introduce a statement of unique certainty and authority — a signal that what follows is an essential, non-negotiable truth about himself, salvation, judgment, or eternal life.

Explanation and Scripture: The double amen in Jewish usage means “so be it” or “truly,” but Jesus’ double amen in the Gospels (especially in John) functions as his own authoritative stamp on teaching. For example, he uses it to introduce the necessity of new birth, John 3:3, and the promise of eternal life for those who hear and believe, John 5:24; he likewise uses it about believing in Christ for life, John 6:47. By contrast, the Synoptic Gospels more often record the single form “Truly I tell you,” as in Matthew 5:18. John 3:3; John 5:24; John 6:47; Matthew 5:18.

Theological significance: The double “amen” points to Jesus’ divine authority as the incarnate Son who speaks God’s decisive revelation (cf. the doctrine of Christ’s authority and the hypostatic union). When Jesus begins with “Truly, truly,” Scripture intends the reader or hearer to take the following claim as foundational Christian truth, not as casual opinion.

Practical application: When you encounter “Verily, verily” in a passage, slow down and treat the sentence as a central claim to believe and obey — for example, read the surrounding context, pray for understanding, and memorize one such verse (try John 5:24 or John 3:3) to meditate on through the week. If you’d like, I can walk through one specific “verily, verily” passage with you and help apply it to your life.
What should we learn from the tribe of Asher?

Briefly: the tribe of Asher teaches that God often gives tangible blessing and favor, but blessing is not a substitute for obedience—prosperity must be stewarded in holiness and used for service. (Genesis 49:20; Deuteronomy 33:24-25)

Key biblical facts and lessons
- God’s blessing and provision: Jacob and Moses both bless Asher, picturing richness and favor—“rich food” and “dipped in oil,” images of abundance, anointing, and hospitality (Genesis 49:20; Deuteronomy 33:24-25). These point to God’s gracious provision and to a vocation to bless others.
- The danger of complacency and compromise: Asher failed to fully drive out the Canaanite inhabitants and often stayed on the coast instead of joining Israel in covenant action (Judges 1:31-32; Judges 5:17). Material blessing without wholehearted obedience led to spiritual compromise.
- A vocational balance: Asher shows both blessing and a call to faithful stewardship—God’s gifts are meant for sanctification and service, not comfort or isolation.

Theological takeaways (brief)
- Stewardship: God’s material blessings are gifts to be stewarded for God’s glory (sanctification and service).
- Sanctification and perseverance: Prosperity must be paired with sustained obedience and corporate faithfulness.
- Fellowship and service: Blessing should produce generosity and participation in the covenant community, not withdrawal.

One concrete next step
- This week, read Deuteronomy 33:24-25 and Judges 1:31-32; ask God to show one area where “Canaanite” compromise has crept into your life (an idol, routine, or relationship). Confess that area, remove one practical enabling factor (for example, limit time, unsubscribe, change the habit), and invite a trustworthy Christian friend to pray and hold you accountable.

If you want, I can give a short 3‑day devotional on Asher that includes Scripture, reflection questions, and practical steps to apply these lessons. Would you like that?
Why does the Bible speak against braided hair?

Short answer: The Bible does not condemn braided hair as inherently sinful, but it warns against elaborate external adornment—including braided hair—when it becomes a sign of pride, ostentation, or a distraction from Christian modesty and the gospel witness, especially in public worship (1 Timothy 2:9-10; 1 Peter 3:3-4).

Why this wording? In the Greco-Roman and Jewish worlds of the New Testament, elaborate hairstyles, jewelry, and expensive dress often signaled social status and could draw attention or even be associated with sexual immorality; Paul’s and Peter’s concern is the heart and public witness, not a blanket rule about hair. See also the discussion of hair and propriety in 1 Corinthians 11:14-15, which treats hair length and honor, not a prohibition against particular styles.

Theological point in one phrase: these passages emphasize biblical modesty and sanctification—internal transformation shown by good works rather than external display.

A practical step: read 1 Timothy 2:9-10 and 1 Peter 3:3-4 in the Berean Standard Bible, ask God to examine your motives, and discuss with a trusted, mature sister or your pastor about how your style of dress and grooming communicates before the church and the world.

What is the difference between a covenant and a contract?

Briefly: a contract is a legal, transactional agreement founded on mutual obligations and enforceable by terms; a covenant is a solemn, often oath‑bound promise that creates a personal, binding relationship of loyalty and faithfulness—especially in Scripture, covenants are how God binds himself to people.

Key differences
- Purpose and character: contracts regulate transactions and exchanges, covenants establish relationships of fidelity and identity (covenantal loyalty versus contractual performance). Deuteronomy 7:9 supports God’s covenant faithfulness; Hebrews 8:6–13 explains the new covenant’s relational character.  
- Who binds whom: contracts usually bind the parties to one another by terms they agree to; biblical covenants often include a sovereign party who freely promises (God) and a people who receive and respond (promise rooted in God’s initiative), see Genesis 17:1–14; Genesis 9:12–13.  
- Ratification and signs: contracts use signatures, witnesses, penalties; biblical covenants are frequently ratified by oaths, blood, or signs (circumcision, rainbow, sacrificial blood), see Exodus 24:8; Genesis 17:10–11; Genesis 9:12–13.  
- Enforcement and endurance: a contract is enforceable by law and ends when obligations are fulfilled or breached; a covenant frames ongoing identity and loyalty and is kept according to the covenant‑maker’s faithfulness (God’s keeping), see Deuteronomy 7:9; Hebrews 8:6–13.  
- Stakes and scope: contracts are conditional (if A then B); biblical covenants often combine promises, obligations, and promises of grace with long‑term or generational scope (Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, New Covenant), see Genesis 9:8–17; Genesis 17:1–14; Matthew 26:28.

Theological significance, briefly
- In Scripture salvation and fellowship with God are presented covenantally, not merely as a business exchange: God binds himself to save and to be faithful, ultimately sealing the promise in Christ’s blood (the new covenant). Hebrews 9:15 and Matthew 26:28 speak to Christ as the mediator and the blood of the covenant; Hebrews 8:6–13 contrasts the old with the better new covenant.

One practical step
- Read Hebrews 8:6–13 and Genesis 17 slowly, asking God to show how his covenant shapes your confidence in him; if you find yourself treating God like a contract‑maker (doing things to earn favor), confess that and lean on his covenantal promise in Christ.

A brief gospel bridge
- The Bible teaches that we are sinners in need of God’s mercy, Christ died and rose to secure forgiveness and to establish the new covenant, and we are invited to repent and trust him for forgiveness and new life (Romans 3:23; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:15). If you’d like, I can help you read those passages or talk through what it means to enter God’s covenant by faith.
If Jesus was God, why did He say "No one is good but God alone"?

Short answer: Jesus was stressing that absolute, moral goodness ultimately belongs to God alone, and he used the man’s address “Good Teacher” to provoke that recognition—without denying his own deity. Because of the incarnation and the hypostatic union (Jesus is fully God and fully man), his speaking as a human teacher to point people to the Father does not contradict that he is God.

Explanation in three brief points:
- Context, plain and pastoral: a rich young ruler called Jesus “Good Teacher,” and Jesus replied, “Why do you call me good? No one is good but God alone,” to get the man to think about what “good” really means and where ultimate goodness comes from (Mark 10:17-18 BSB). Jesus’ question presses the hearer to acknowledge God’s unique holiness.
- Theological balance: Christians confess that in the incarnation the second Person of the Trinity took on human nature (hypostatic union), yet remained fully God. So when Jesus affirms that only God is truly good he is both pointing to the Father’s unique goodness and, implicitly, inviting the conclusion that if Jesus is truly “good” in that ultimate sense, he is God (see John 1:1-14 BSB; John 10:30 BSB; Philippians 2:6-8 BSB).
- Practical pastoral point: Jesus’ answer exposes our need and should lead us to trust him not merely as a wise teacher but as Lord and Savior who alone can save and make us truly good.

Relevant passages: Mark 10:17-18 BSB; John 1:1-14 BSB; John 10:30 BSB; Philippians 2:6-8 BSB.

If you want a short Gospel invitation: The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory, and that Jesus, God incarnate, died and rose for sinners so that whoever repents and trusts him will be forgiven and given new life (Romans 3:23 BSB; Romans 6:23 BSB; Romans 10:9-10 BSB).
